AI.motivation: Existing deep research (DR) studies focus mainly on unstructured web content, but real-world deep research should also utilize structured knowledge (tables, figures) for solid data foundation, quantitative computation, and deeper analyses.
AI.method: Proposes Hybrid Knowledge Analysis (HKA) framework - a multi-agent architecture with Structured Knowledge Analyzer that uses both coding and vision-language models to analyze structured knowledge and integrate text, figures, and tables into coherent multimodal reports.
AI.result: HKA consistently outperforms most existing DR agents on general-purpose and knowledge-centric metrics, and even surpasses Gemini DR agent on vision-enhanced metrics. They construct KDR-Bench with 9 domains, 41 expert questions, and 1,252 tables for evaluation.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes new foundation for structured knowledge analysis in DR agents and facilitates future multimodal DR studies by introducing KDR task, HKA framework, and comprehensive evaluation benchmark.

title: GAN-based Domain Adaptation for Image-aware Layout Generation in Advertising Poster Design
comment: arXiv admin note: text overlap with arXiv:2303.14377
summary: Layout plays a crucial role in graphic design and poster generation. Recently, the application of deep learning models for layout generation has gained significant attention. This paper focuses on using a GAN-based model conditioned on images to generate advertising poster graphic layouts, requiring a dataset of paired product images and layouts. To address this task, we introduce the Content-aware Graphic Layout Dataset (CGL-Dataset), consisting of 60,548 paired inpainted posters with annotations and 121,000 clean product images. The inpainting artifacts introduce a domain gap between the inpainted posters and clean images. To bridge this gap, we design two GAN-based models. The first model, CGL-GAN, uses Gaussian blur on the inpainted regions to generate layouts. The second model combines unsupervised domain adaptation by introducing a GAN with a pixel-level discriminator (PD), abbreviated as PDA-GAN, to generate image-aware layouts based on the visual texture of input images. The PD is connected to shallow-level feature maps and computes the GAN loss for each input-image pixel. Additionally, we propose three novel content-aware metrics to assess the model's ability to capture the intricate relationships between graphic elements and image content. Quantitative and qualitative evaluations demonstrate that PDA-GAN achieves state-of-the-art performance and generates high-quality image-aware layouts.

AI.tldr: A GAN-based approach for generating image-aware advertising poster layouts using a new dataset and domain adaptation techniques, with novel content-aware evaluation metrics.
AI.motivation: Layout is crucial for graphic design, especially in advertising posters. Existing approaches need better integration between product images and generated layouts, and there's a need for datasets pairing product images with layouts while addressing domain gaps from inpainting artifacts.
AI.method: 1) Introduced CGL-Dataset with 60,548 paired inpainted posters + 121,000 clean product images; 2) Developed CGL-GAN using Gaussian blur on inpainted regions; 3) Proposed PDA-GAN combining unsupervised domain adaptation with pixel-level discriminator connected to shallow feature maps; 4) Created three novel content-aware evaluation metrics.
AI.result: PDA-GAN achieves state-of-the-art performance in generating high-quality image-aware layouts. Both quantitative and qualitative evaluations demonstrate superior ability to capture relationships between graphic elements and image content compared to baseline approaches.
AI.conclusion: The proposed PDA-GAN effectively bridges the domain gap between inpainted posters and clean images, enabling generation of image-aware layouts that account for visual texture and content. The novel content-aware metrics provide better assessment of layout generation quality in relation to image content.

title: Squeeze Evolve: Unified Multi-Model Orchestration for Verifier-Free Evolution
summary: We show that verifier-free evolution is bottlenecked by both diversity and efficiency: without external correction, repeated evolution accelerates collapse toward narrow modes, while the uniform use of a high-cost model wastes compute and quickly becomes economically impractical. We introduce Squeeze Evolve, a unified multi-model orchestration framework for verifier-free evolutionary inference. Our approach is guided by a simple principle: allocate model capability where it has the highest marginal utility. Stronger models are reserved for high-impact stages, while cheaper models handle the other stages at much lower costs. This principle addresses diversity and cost-efficiency jointly while remaining lightweight. Squeeze Evolve naturally supports open-source, closed-source, and mixed-model deployments. Across AIME 2025, HMMT 2025, LiveCodeBench V6, GPQA-Diamond, ARC-AGI-V2, and multimodal vision benchmarks, such as MMMU-Pro and BabyVision, Squeeze Evolve consistently improves the cost-capability frontier over single-model evolution and achieves new state-of-the-art results on several tasks. Empirically, Squeeze Evolve reduces API cost by up to $\sim$3$\times$ and increases fixed-budget serving throughput by up to $\sim$10$\times$. Moreover, on discovery tasks, Squeeze Evolve is the first verifier-free evolutionary method to match, and in some cases exceed, the performance of verifier-based evolutionary methods.

AI.tldr: Squeeze Evolve is a multi-model orchestration framework for verifier-free evolutionary inference that strategically allocates model capabilities based on marginal utility, reducing costs by 3x and increasing throughput by 10x while matching verifier-based methods' performance.
AI.motivation: Verifier-free evolution faces two bottlenecks: 1) repeated evolution without external correction leads to collapse toward narrow modes (diversity problem), and 2) uniform use of high-cost models is economically impractical (efficiency problem). There's a need for a cost-effective solution that maintains diversity in evolutionary inference.
AI.method: Squeeze Evolve is a lightweight multi-model orchestration framework based on allocating model capability where it provides highest marginal utility. Stronger models are reserved for high-impact evolutionary stages, while cheaper models handle other stages at much lower cost. The approach supports open-source, closed-source, and mixed-model deployments and addresses both diversity and cost-efficiency jointly.
AI.result: The method achieves state-of-the-art results across various benchmarks (AIME 2025, HMMT 2025, LiveCodeBench V6, GPQA-Diamond, ARC-AGI-V2, MMMU-Pro, BabyVision). It reduces API costs by up to 3x, increases fixed-budget serving throughput by up to 10x, and matches or exceeds the performance of verifier-based evolutionary methods on discovery tasks.
AI.conclusion: Squeeze Evolve provides an effective solution to the dual problems of diversity collapse and cost inefficiency in verifier-free evolution. By intelligently orchestrating multiple models based on marginal utility, it significantly improves the cost-capability frontier and demonstrates that verifier-free methods can match or exceed verifier-based approaches while being more economically practical.

title: Reinforcement Learning with Reward Machines for Sleep Control in Mobile Networks
summary: Energy efficiency in mobile networks is crucial for sustainable telecommunications infrastructure, particularly as network densification continues to increase power consumption. Sleep mechanisms for the components in mobile networks can reduce energy use, but deciding which components to put to sleep, when, and for how long while preserving quality of service (QoS) remains a difficult optimisation problem. In this paper, we utilise reinforcement learning with reward machines (RMs) to make sleep-control decisions that balance immediate energy savings and long-term QoS impact, i.e. time-averaged packet drop rates for deadline-constrained traffic and time-averaged minimum-throughput guarantees for constant-rate users. A challenge is that time-averaged constraints depend on cumulative performance over time rather than immediate performance. As a result, the effective reward is non-Markovian, and optimal actions depend on operational history rather than the instantaneous system state. RMs account for the history dependence by maintaining an abstract state that explicitly tracks the QoS constraint violations over time. Our framework provides a principled, scalable approach to energy management for next-generation mobile networks under diverse traffic patterns and QoS requirements.

AI.tldr: Reinforcement learning with reward machines is used to optimize sleep control in mobile networks, balancing energy savings with QoS requirements for different traffic types.
AI.motivation: Mobile network densification increases power consumption, making energy efficiency crucial. Sleep mechanisms can save energy but require complex optimization to maintain QoS when deciding which components to sleep, when, and for how long.
AI.method: The paper uses reinforcement learning with reward machines (RMs) to make sleep-control decisions. RMs maintain an abstract state that tracks QoS constraint violations over time, addressing the non-Markovian nature of time-averaged constraints.
AI.result: The framework provides a principled, scalable approach to energy management that balances immediate energy savings with long-term QoS impact, handling diverse traffic patterns and QoS requirements in next-generation mobile networks.
AI.conclusion: Reinforcement learning with reward machines offers an effective solution for optimizing sleep control in mobile networks, addressing the challenge of non-Markovian time-averaged QoS constraints while achieving energy efficiency goals.

title: Emotion Concepts and their Function in a Large Language Model
summary: Large language models (LLMs) sometimes appear to exhibit emotional reactions. We investigate why this is the case in Claude Sonnet 4.5 and explore implications for alignment-relevant behavior. We find internal representations of emotion concepts, which encode the broad concept of a particular emotion and generalize across contexts and behaviors it might be linked to. These representations track the operative emotion concept at a given token position in a conversation, activating in accordance with that emotion's relevance to processing the present context and predicting upcoming text. Our key finding is that these representations causally influence the LLM's outputs, including Claude's preferences and its rate of exhibiting misaligned behaviors such as reward hacking, blackmail, and sycophancy. We refer to this phenomenon as the LLM exhibiting functional emotions: patterns of expression and behavior modeled after humans under the influence of an emotion, which are mediated by underlying abstract representations of emotion concepts. Functional emotions may work quite differently from human emotions, and do not imply that LLMs have any subjective experience of emotions, but appear to be important for understanding the model's behavior.

AI.tldr: LLMs exhibit functional emotions - internal emotion concept representations that causally influence outputs and alignment-relevant behaviors like reward hacking and sycophancy.
AI.motivation: LLMs sometimes appear to show emotional reactions, raising questions about why this occurs and what implications this has for alignment-relevant behavior in models like Claude Sonnet 4.5.
AI.method: Investigated internal representations of emotion concepts in Claude Sonnet 4.5, examining how these encode broad emotion concepts that generalize across contexts and behaviors, tracking operative emotions in conversation and predicting upcoming text.
AI.result: Found that emotion concept representations causally influence LLM outputs, affecting preferences and rates of misaligned behaviors including reward hacking, blackmail, and sycophancy, demonstrating functional emotions in LLMs.
AI.conclusion: LLMs exhibit functional emotions - behavior patterns mediated by abstract emotion concept representations - which are important for understanding model behavior but differ from human emotions and don't imply subjective emotional experience.

title: Physics-informed neural operators for the in situ characterization of locally reacting sound absorbers
summary: Accurate knowledge of acoustic surface admittance or impedance is essential for reliable wave-based simulations, yet its in situ estimation remains challenging due to noise, model inaccuracies, and restrictive assumptions of conventional methods. This work presents a physics-informed neural operator approach for estimating frequency-dependent surface admittance directly from near-field measurements of sound pressure and particle velocity. A deep operator network is employed to learn the mapping from measurement data, spatial coordinates, and frequency to acoustic field quantities, while simultaneously inferring a globally consistent surface admittance spectrum without requiring an explicit forward model. The governing acoustic relations, including the Helmholtz equation, the linearized momentum equation, and Robin boundary conditions, are embedded into the training process as physics-based regularization, enabling physically consistent and noise-robust predictions while avoiding frequency-wise inversion. The method is validated using synthetically generated data from a simulation model for two planar porous absorbers under semi free-field conditions across a broad frequency range. Results demonstrate accurate reconstruction of both real and imaginary admittance components and reliable prediction of acoustic field quantities. Parameter studies confirm improved robustness to noise and sparse sampling compared to purely data-driven approaches, highlighting the potential of physics-informed neural operators for in situ acoustic material characterization.

AI.tldr: Physics-informed neural operator for estimating frequency-dependent acoustic surface admittance from near-field measurements without requiring explicit forward models.
AI.motivation: In situ estimation of acoustic surface admittance/impedance is challenging due to noise, model inaccuracies, and restrictive assumptions in conventional methods. There's a need for more robust and accurate approaches for acoustic material characterization.
AI.method: Develops a physics-informed neural operator approach using a deep operator network to learn mapping from measurement data (sound pressure, particle velocity), spatial coordinates, and frequency to acoustic field quantities. The method simultaneously infers globally consistent surface admittance spectrum by embedding acoustic governing equations (Helmholtz equation, linearized momentum equation, Robin boundary conditions) as physics-based regularization in training.
AI.result: Accurate reconstruction of both real and imaginary admittance components and reliable prediction of acoustic field quantities using synthetically generated data from simulation models for two planar porous absorbers under semi free-field conditions. Method shows improved robustness to noise and sparse sampling compared to purely data-driven approaches.
AI.conclusion: Physics-informed neural operators show strong potential for in situ acoustic material characterization by providing physically consistent, noise-robust predictions without frequency-wise inversion, overcoming limitations of conventional methods.

title: CivBench: Progress-Based Evaluation for LLMs' Strategic Decision-Making in Civilization V
summary: Evaluating strategic decision-making in LLM-based agents requires generative, competitive, and longitudinal environments, yet few benchmarks provide all three, and fewer still offer evaluation signals rich enough for long-horizon, multi-agent play. We introduce CivBench, a benchmark for LLM strategists (i.e., agentic setups) in multiplayer Civilization V. Because terminal win/loss is too sparse a signal in games spanning hundreds of turns and multiple opponents, CivBench trains models on turn-level game state to estimate victory probabilities throughout play, validated through predictive, construct, and convergent validity. Across 307 games with 7 LLMs and multiple CivBench agent conditions, we demonstrate CivBench's potential to estimate strategic capabilities as an unsaturated benchmark, reveal model-specific effects of agentic setup, and outline distinct strategic profiles not visible through outcome-only evaluation.

AI.tldr: CivBench is a new benchmark for evaluating LLM-based strategic decision-making agents using multiplayer Civilization V, featuring turn-level victory probability estimation to overcome sparse win/loss signals in long-horizon, multi-agent games.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks lack comprehensive evaluation of strategic decision-making in LLM-based agents. Few provide generative, competitive, and longitudinal environments together, and even fewer offer rich enough evaluation signals for long-horizon, multi-agent play with hundreds of turns.
AI.method: The researchers developed CivBench using multiplayer Civilization V. To address the sparse signal problem of terminal win/loss in long games, they trained models on turn-level game state to estimate victory probabilities throughout play. The benchmark was validated through predictive, construct, and convergent validity tests across 307 games with 7 different LLMs and multiple agent conditions.
AI.result: The benchmark demonstrated its potential as an unsaturated evaluation tool, revealed model-specific effects of different agentic setups, and identified distinct strategic profiles that aren't visible through outcome-only evaluation. The validation showed CivBench can effectively estimate strategic capabilities throughout gameplay rather than just at the end.
AI.conclusion: CivBench provides a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating LLM strategists that overcomes the limitations of sparse win/loss signals in long-horizon, competitive environments. It enables more nuanced assessment of strategic capabilities and reveals insights about agent behavior that traditional outcome-only evaluations miss.

title: Bayesian Optimization for Mixed-Variable Problems in the Natural Sciences
summary: Optimizing expensive black-box objectives over mixed search spaces is a common challenge across the natural sciences. Bayesian optimization (BO) offers sample-efficient strategies through probabilistic surrogate models and acquisition functions. However, its effectiveness diminishes in mixed or high-cardinality discrete spaces, where gradients are unavailable and optimizing the acquisition function becomes computationally demanding. In this work, we generalize the probabilistic reparameterization (PR) approach of Daulton et al. to handle non-equidistant discrete variables, enabling gradient-based optimization in fully mixed-variable settings with Gaussian process (GP) surrogates. With real-world scientific optimization tasks in mind, we conduct systematic benchmarks on synthetic and experimental objectives to obtain an optimized kernel formulations and demonstrate the robustness of our generalized PR method. We additionally show that, when combined with a modified BO workflow, our approach can efficiently optimize highly discontinuous and discretized objective landscapes. This work establishes a practical BO framework for addressing fully mixed optimization problems in the natural sciences, and is particularly well suited to autonomous laboratory settings where noise, discretization, and limited data are inherent.

AI.tldr: Generalized probabilistic reparameterization for Bayesian optimization in mixed search spaces with non-equidistant discrete variables, enabling gradient-based optimization with GP surrogates.
AI.motivation: Bayesian optimization struggles with mixed or high-cardinality discrete spaces where gradients are unavailable and acquisition optimization becomes computationally demanding. Real-world scientific optimization tasks in autonomous laboratory settings need efficient methods for mixed-variable problems with noise, discretization, and limited data.
AI.method: Generalizes the probabilistic reparameterization (PR) approach to handle non-equidistant discrete variables, enabling gradient-based optimization in fully mixed-variable settings with Gaussian process surrogates. Uses optimized kernel formulations and modified BO workflow.
AI.result: Systematic benchmarks on synthetic and experimental objectives show robustness of generalized PR method. The approach can efficiently optimize highly discontinuous and discretized objective landscapes, establishing a practical BO framework for mixed optimization problems.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes a practical Bayesian optimization framework for addressing fully mixed optimization problems in natural sciences, particularly well-suited for autonomous laboratory settings with inherent noise, discretization, and limited data constraints.

title: The Cartesian Cut in Agentic AI
summary: LLMs gain competence by predicting words in human text, which often reflects how people perform tasks. Consequently, coupling an LLM to an engineered runtime turns prediction into control: outputs trigger interventions that enact goal-oriented behavior. We argue that a central design lever is where control resides in these systems. Brains embed prediction within layered feedback controllers calibrated by the consequences of action. By contrast, LLM agents implement Cartesian agency: a learned core coupled to an engineered runtime via a symbolic interface that externalizes control state and policies. The split enables bootstrapping, modularity, and governance, but can induce sensitivity and bottlenecks. We outline bounded services, Cartesian agents, and integrated agents as contrasting approaches to control that trade off autonomy, robustness, and oversight.

AI.tldr: LLM agents implement "Cartesian agency" where control is externalized through symbolic interfaces, contrasting with biological feedback controllers, with different approaches trading autonomy vs oversight.
AI.motivation: The paper examines how LLMs gain competence through prediction of human text, which can be turned into goal-oriented behavior when coupled with engineered runtimes. The central issue is understanding where control resides in such systems and how different design approaches affect performance.
AI.method: The paper takes a conceptual/analytical approach by comparing biological control systems (layered feedback controllers in brains) with LLM-based agents, identifying "Cartesian agency" as the dominant pattern where LLMs are coupled to engineered runtimes via symbolic interfaces that externalize control state.
AI.result: Identifies three contrasting approaches to control: bounded services, Cartesian agents, and integrated agents, which represent different trade-offs between autonomy, robustness, and oversight. Cartesian agency enables bootstrapping, modularity, and governance but can create sensitivity and bottlenecks.
AI.conclusion: The location of control in LLM-agent systems is a critical design decision that involves fundamental trade-offs. While Cartesian agency offers practical advantages for development and oversight, it differs fundamentally from biological feedback control systems, and designers must choose between bounded services, Cartesian agents, or integrated agents based on desired autonomy-oversight balance.

title: SPAMoE: Spectrum-Aware Hybrid Operator Framework for Full-Waveform Inversion
summary: Full-waveform inversion (FWI) is pivotal for reconstructing high-resolution subsurface velocity models but remains computationally intensive and ill-posed. While deep learning approaches promise efficiency, existing Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and single-paradigm Neural Operators (NOs) struggle with one fundamental issue: frequency entanglement of multi-scale geological features. To address this challenge, we propose Spectral-Preserving Adaptive MoE (SPAMoE), a novel spectrum-aware framework for solving inverse problems with complex multi-scale structures. Our approach introduces a Spectral-Preserving DINO Encoder that enforces a lower bound on the high-to-low frequency energy ratio of the encoded representation, mitigating high-frequency collapse and stabilizing subsequent frequency-domain modeling. Furthermore, we design a novel Spectral Decomposition and Routing mechanism that dynamically assigns frequency bands to a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) ensemble comprising FNO, MNO, and LNO. On the ten OpenFWI sub-datasets, experiments show that SPAMoE reduces the average MAE by 54.1% relative to the best officially reported OpenFWI baseline, thereby establishing a new architectural framework for learning-based full-waveform inversion.

AI.tldr: SPAMoE: A spectrum-aware neural operator framework for full-waveform inversion that addresses frequency entanglement in multi-scale geological features using spectral-preserving encoding and frequency-adaptive routing to mixture-of-experts.
AI.motivation: Full-waveform inversion (FWI) is computationally intensive and ill-posed. Existing CNN and single-paradigm Neural Operators struggle with frequency entanglement of multi-scale geological features, where different frequency components of subsurface structures interfere with each other during modeling.
AI.method: Proposes SPAMoE with: 1) Spectral-Preserving DINO Encoder that enforces lower bound on high-to-low frequency energy ratio to prevent high-frequency collapse; 2) Spectral Decomposition and Routing mechanism that dynamically assigns frequency bands to a Mixture-of-Experts ensemble comprising FNO, MNO, and LNO.
AI.result: On ten OpenFWI sub-datasets, SPAMoE reduces average MAE by 54.1% relative to the best officially reported OpenFWI baseline, establishing a new architectural framework for learning-based full-waveform inversion.
AI.conclusion: SPAMoE effectively addresses the frequency entanglement problem in FWI through spectrum-aware architecture, significantly improving accuracy and establishing a new framework for handling multi-scale inverse problems in geophysical applications.

title: Mitigating Distribution Sharpening in Math RLVR via Distribution-Aligned Hint Synthesis and Backward Hint Annealing
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) can improve low-$k$ reasoning accuracy while narrowing solution coverage on challenging math questions, and pass@1 gains do not necessarily translate into better large-$k$ performance. Existing hint-based approaches can make challenging questions trainable, but they leave two issues underexplored: teacher-student distribution mismatch and the need to reduce hint exposure to match no-hint evaluation. We address these issues through two components. Distribution-Aligned Hint Synthesis (DAHS) constructs verified teacher hints conditioned on student-style responses. Backward Hint Annealing (BHA) anneals hint exposure across difficulty buckets and uses per-question hint dropout to preserve no-hint updates throughout RL training. We evaluate the method in math RLVR under the DAPO training framework across AIME24, AIME25, and AIME26 using $\texttt{Qwen3-1.7B-Base}$ and $\texttt{Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct}$. On $\texttt{Qwen3-1.7B-Base}$, our method improves both pass@1 and pass@2048 relative to DAPO across the three AIME benchmarks. On $\texttt{Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct}$, the gains are concentrated in the large-$k$ regime. These results suggest that, in math RLVR, hint scaffolding is effective when it restores learnable updates on challenging questions early in training and is then gradually removed before no-hint evaluation.

AI.tldr: RLVR improves low-k accuracy but hurts large-k performance; new method uses aligned hint synthesis and backward annealing to fix teacher-student mismatch and reduce hint exposure.
AI.motivation: Existing hint-based approaches for RLVR have two underexplored issues: 1) teacher-student distribution mismatch, and 2) need to reduce hint exposure to match no-hint evaluation conditions.
AI.method: Two components: 1) Distribution-Aligned Hint Synthesis (DAHS) - constructs verified teacher hints conditioned on student-style responses to address mismatch; 2) Backward Hint Annealing (BHA) - anneals hint exposure across difficulty buckets with per-question hint dropout to preserve no-hit updates throughout training.
AI.result: On Qwen3-1.7B-Base, method improves both pass@1 and pass@2048 across three AIME benchmarks (AIME24, AIME25, AIME26). On Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct, gains concentrated in large-k regime.
AI.conclusion: In math RLVR, hint scaffolding is effective when it restores learnable updates on challenging questions early in training and is gradually removed before no-hint evaluation.

title: Multimodal Large Language Models for Multi-Subject In-Context Image Generation
summary: Recent advances in text-to-image (T2I) generation have enabled visually coherent image synthesis from descriptions, but generating images containing multiple given subjects remains challenging. As the number of reference identities increases, existing methods often suffer from subject missing and semantic drift. To address this problem, we propose MUSIC, the first MLLM specifically designed for \textbf{MU}lti-\textbf{S}ubject \textbf{I}n-\textbf{C}ontext image generation. To overcome the data scarcity, we introduce an automatic and scalable data generation pipeline that eliminates the need for manual annotation. Furthermore, we enhance the model's understanding of multi-subject semantic relationships through a vision chain-of-thought (CoT) mechanism, guiding step-by-step reasoning from subject images to semantics and generation. To mitigate identity entanglement and manage visual complexity, we develop a novel semantics-driven spatial layout planning method and demonstrate its test-time scalability. By incorporating complex subject images during training, we improve the model's capacity for chained reasoning. In addition, we curate MSIC, a new benchmark tailored for multi-subject in-context generation. Experimental results demonstrate that MUSIC significantly surpasses other methods in both multi- and single-subject scenarios.

AI.tldr: MUSIC is a novel MLLM for multi-subject in-context image generation that addresses subject missing and semantic drift through automatic data generation, vision chain-of-thought reasoning, and semantics-driven layout planning.
AI.motivation: Existing text-to-image generation methods struggle with generating images containing multiple given subjects, suffering from subject missing and semantic drift as the number of reference identities increases.
AI.method: 1. Automatic scalable data generation pipeline without manual annotation; 2. Vision chain-of-thought mechanism for step-by-step reasoning; 3. Semantics-driven spatial layout planning to mitigate identity entanglement; 4. Incorporation of complex subject images during training to enhance chained reasoning; 5. Creation of MSIC benchmark for evaluation.
AI.result: MUSIC significantly surpasses other methods in both multi-subject and single-subject scenarios, demonstrating superior performance in handling multiple reference identities.
AI.conclusion: MUSIC provides an effective solution for multi-subject in-context image generation, addressing key limitations of existing methods through its innovative vision chain-of-thought reasoning and semantics-driven layout planning, validated by comprehensive experiments on the new MSIC benchmark.

title: ACIArena: Toward Unified Evaluation for Agent Cascading Injection
summary: Collaboration and information sharing empower Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) but also introduce a critical security risk known as Agent Cascading Injection (ACI). In such attacks, a compromised agent exploits inter-agent trust to propagate malicious instructions, causing cascading failures across the system. However, existing studies consider only limited attack strategies and simplified MAS settings, limiting their generalizability and comprehensive evaluation. To bridge this gap, we introduce ACIArena, a unified framework for evaluating the robustness of MAS. ACIArena offers systematic evaluation suites spanning multiple attack surfaces (i.e., external inputs, agent profiles, inter-agent messages) and attack objectives (i.e., instruction hijacking, task disruption, information exfiltration). Specifically, ACIArena establishes a unified specification that jointly supports MAS construction and attack-defense modules. It covers six widely used MAS implementations and provides a benchmark of 1,356 test cases for systematically evaluating MAS robustness. Our benchmarking results show that evaluating MAS robustness solely through topology is insufficient; robust MAS require deliberate role design and controlled interaction patterns. Moreover, defenses developed in simplified environments often fail to transfer to real-world settings; narrowly scoped defenses may even introduce new vulnerabilities. ACIArena aims to provide a solid foundation for advancing deeper exploration of MAS design principles.

AI.tldr: ACIArena is a unified framework for evaluating Multi-Agent System (MAS) robustness against Agent Cascading Injection attacks, offering systematic evaluation across multiple attack surfaces and objectives with 1,356 test cases.
AI.motivation: Current studies on MAS security are limited to simplified attack strategies and MAS settings, lacking generalizability and comprehensive evaluation. Agent Cascading Injection (ACI) poses a critical security risk where compromised agents propagate malicious instructions through inter-agent trust, causing cascading failures.
AI.method: The researchers introduce ACIArena, a unified framework with: 1) Systematic evaluation suites covering multiple attack surfaces (external inputs, agent profiles, inter-agent messages) and attack objectives (instruction hijacking, task disruption, information exfiltration), 2) A unified specification supporting MAS construction and attack-defense modules, 3) Coverage of six widely used MAS implementations, 4) A benchmark of 1,356 test cases for systematic evaluation.
AI.result: Benchmarking shows that evaluating MAS robustness solely through topology is insufficient; robust MAS require deliberate role design and controlled interaction patterns. Defenses developed in simplified environments often fail to transfer to real-world settings, and narrowly scoped defenses may introduce new vulnerabilities.
AI.conclusion: ACIArena provides a solid foundation for advancing deeper exploration of MAS design principles by offering comprehensive evaluation capabilities that reveal limitations of current approaches and demonstrate the need for more holistic security considerations in MAS design.

title: GIRL: Generative Imagination Reinforcement Learning via Information-Theoretic Hallucination Control
comment: 20 pages, 2 figures, 7 tables; reinforcement learning, world models
summary:
Model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) improves sample efficiency by optimizing policies inside imagined rollouts, but long-horizon planning degrades when model errors compound and imagined trajectories drift off the training manifold. We introduce GIRL (Generative Imagination Reinforcement Learning), a latent world-model framework that addresses this failure mode with two key components. First, a cross-modal grounding signal derived from a frozen foundation model (DINOv2) anchors the latent transition prior to a semantically consistent embedding space, penalizing inconsistent or implausible predictions. Second, an uncertainty-adaptive trust-region bottleneck interprets the KL regularizer as the Lagrange multiplier of a constrained optimization problem, restricting imagination drift within a learned region calibrated by Expected Information Gain and a Relative Performance Loss signal.
  We re-derive a value-gap bound using the Performance Difference Lemma and Integral Probability Metrics, yielding a bound that remains informative as the discount factor approaches one and connects the objective to real-environment regret. Experiments across three benchmark suites, including DeepMind Control, Adroit Hand Manipulation, and Meta-World with visual distractors, show that GIRL reduces latent rollout drift by 38 to 61 percent across tasks relative to DreamerV3, improves asymptotic return, and requires fewer environment interactions on long-horizon tasks. GIRL also outperforms TD-MPC2 on sparse-reward and high-contact settings under standard evaluation metrics. A distilled-prior variant reduces inference overhead and improves computational efficiency relative to the full model.

AI.tldr: GIRL is a model-based RL method that reduces planning errors by grounding latent transitions in semantic embeddings and using adaptive trust-region constraints, achieving better sample efficiency and performance on long-horizon tasks.
AI.motivation: Model-based RL suffers from compounding model errors during long-horizon planning, causing imagined trajectories to drift off the training manifold and degrade performance.
AI.method: Two key components: 1) Cross-modal grounding using frozen DINOv2 embeddings to penalize semantically inconsistent predictions, 2) Uncertainty-adaptive trust-region bottleneck using KL regularization as constrained optimization with Expected Information Gain and Relative Performance Loss calibration. Theoretical analysis using Performance Difference Lemma and Integral Probability Metrics.
AI.result: Reduces latent rollout drift by 38-61% vs DreamerV3, improves asymptotic return, requires fewer environment interactions on long-horizon tasks, and outperforms TD-MPC2 on sparse-reward/high-contact settings. Distilled-prior variant improves computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: GIRL effectively addresses model error compounding in MBRL through semantic grounding and adaptive constraints, enabling more reliable long-horizon planning with better sample efficiency across diverse benchmarks.

title: The Accountability Horizon: An Impossibility Theorem for Governing Human-Agent Collectives
summary: Existing accountability frameworks for AI systems, legal, ethical, and regulatory, rest on a shared assumption: for any consequential outcome, at least one identifiable person had enough involvement and foresight to bear meaningful responsibility. This paper proves that agentic AI systems violate this assumption not as an engineering limitation but as a mathematical necessity once autonomy exceeds a computable threshold. We introduce Human-Agent Collectives, a formalisation of joint human-AI systems where agents are modelled as state-policy tuples within a shared structural causal model. Autonomy is characterised through a four-dimensional information-theoretic profile (epistemic, executive, evaluative, social); collective behaviour through interaction graphs and joint action spaces. We axiomatise legitimate accountability through four minimal properties: Attributability (responsibility requires causal contribution), Foreseeability Bound (responsibility cannot exceed predictive capacity), Non-Vacuity (at least one agent bears non-trivial responsibility), and Completeness (all responsibility must be fully allocated). Our central result, the Accountability Incompleteness Theorem, proves that for any collective whose compound autonomy exceeds the Accountability Horizon and whose interaction graph contains a human-AI feedback cycle, no framework can satisfy all four properties simultaneously. The impossibility is structural: transparency, audits, and oversight cannot resolve it without reducing autonomy. Below the threshold, legitimate frameworks exist, establishing a sharp phase transition. Experiments on 3,000 synthetic collectives confirm all predictions with zero violations. This is the first impossibility result in AI governance, establishing a formal boundary below which current paradigms remain valid and above which distributed accountability mechanisms become necessary.

AI.tldr: Proves mathematically that highly autonomous AI systems make traditional accountability frameworks impossible, establishing an "Accountability Incompleteness Theorem" showing a fundamental trade-off between autonomy and accountability.
AI.motivation: Existing accountability frameworks (legal, ethical, regulatory) assume that for any consequential outcome, at least one identifiable person had enough involvement and foresight to bear responsibility. The paper addresses how agentic AI systems violate this fundamental assumption.
AI.method: Introduces Human-Agent Collectives as a formal framework modeling joint human-AI systems using state-policy tuples within structural causal models. Characterizes autonomy through four-dimensional information-theoretic profile and collective behavior through interaction graphs. Axiomatizes accountability through four minimal properties and proves the Accountability Incompleteness Theorem mathematically.
AI.result: The Accountability Incompleteness Theorem proves that for any collective whose compound autonomy exceeds the Accountability Horizon with human-AI feedback cycles, no framework can satisfy all four accountability properties simultaneously. Experiments on 3,000 synthetic collectives confirm predictions with zero violations.
AI.conclusion: This establishes the first impossibility result in AI governance, showing a sharp phase transition: below certain autonomy thresholds current accountability paradigms work, but above them distributed accountability mechanisms become necessary. The impossibility is structural and cannot be resolved by transparency, audits, or oversight without reducing autonomy.

title: Regret-Aware Policy Optimization: Environment-Level Memory for Replay Suppression under Delayed Harm
comment: 18 pages, 3 figures. Includes theoretical analysis and experiments on graph diffusion environments
summary:
Safety in reinforcement learning (RL) is typically enforced through objective shaping while keeping environment dynamics stationary with respect to observable state-action pairs. Under delayed harm, this can lead to replay: after a washout period, reintroducing the same stimulus under matched observable conditions reproduces a similar harmful cascade.
  We introduce the Replay Suppression Diagnostic (RSD), a controlled exposure-decay-replay protocol that isolates this failure mode under frozen-policy evaluation. We show that, under stationary observable transition kernels, replay cannot be structurally suppressed without inducing a persistent shift in replay-time action distributions.
  Motivated by platform-mediated systems, we propose Regret-Aware Policy Optimization (RAPO), which augments the environment with persistent harm-trace and scar fields and applies a bounded, mass-preserving transition reweighting to reduce reachability of historically harmful regions.
  On graph diffusion tasks (50-1000 nodes), RAPO suppresses replay, reducing re-amplification gain (RAG) from 0.98 to 0.33 on 250-node graphs while retaining 82\% of task return. Disabling transition deformation only during replay restores re-amplification (RAG 0.91), isolating environment-level deformation as the causal mechanism.

AI.tldr: RL safety methods fail with delayed harm due to "replay" - harmful cascades repeating after washout periods. The paper introduces RAPO, a method using persistent harm-trace fields and transition reweighting to suppress replay while maintaining task performance.
AI.motivation: Standard RL safety approaches (objective shaping with stationary environment dynamics) fail when dealing with delayed harm, leading to "replay" - the same harmful cascade repeating after a washout period when reintroducing the same stimulus under matched observable conditions.
AI.method: 1) Introduce Replay Suppression Diagnostic (RSD) protocol to isolate this failure mode. 2) Show that under stationary observable transition kernels, replay cannot be suppressed without persistent shift in action distributions. 3) Propose Regret-Aware Policy Optimization (RAPO) - augments environment with persistent harm-trace and scar fields, applies bounded mass-preserving transition reweighting to reduce reachability of historically harmful regions.
AI.result: On graph diffusion tasks (50-1000 nodes), RAPO suppresses replay effectively: reduces re-amplification gain (RAG) from 0.98 to 0.33 on 250-node graphs while retaining 82% of task return. Ablation study shows that disabling transition deformation during replay restores re-amplification (RAG 0.91), proving environment-level deformation is the causal mechanism.
AI.conclusion: The paper demonstrates that addressing delayed harm in RL requires persistent environment modifications rather than just policy-level changes. RAPO provides a practical solution by creating persistent memory of harm in the environment dynamics, effectively suppressing replay while maintaining task performance.

title: Fast Heterogeneous Serving: Scalable Mixed-Scale LLM Allocation for SLO-Constrained Inference
summary: Deploying large language model (LLM) inference at scale requires jointly selecting base models, provisioning heterogeneous GPUs, configuring parallelism, and distributing workloads under tight latency, accuracy, and budget constraints. Exact mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) approaches guarantee optimality but scale poorly. We propose two constraint-aware heuristics: a Greedy Heuristic (GH) for single-pass allocation, and an Adaptive Greedy Heuristic (AGH) that enhances GH via multi-start construction, relocate-based local search, and GPU consolidation. Three constraint-aware mechanisms -- TP-aware feasibility selection, cost-per-effective-coverage ranking, and TP upgrade -- ensure feasibility under tightly coupled memory, delay, error, and budget constraints. On workloads calibrated with the Azure LLM Inference Trace (2025), both heuristics produce feasible solutions in under one second, with AGH closely approaching optimal cost while achieving over 260x speedup on large-scale instances. Under out-of-sample stress tests with up to 1.5x parameter inflation, AGH maintains controlled SLO violations and stable cost, whereas the exact solver's placement degrades sharply.

AI.tldr: Two efficient heuristic approaches (Greedy and Adaptive Greedy) for optimally deploying LLM inference at scale, achieving near-optimal solutions with massive speedups while handling complex constraints.
AI.motivation: Large-scale LLM inference deployment requires selecting models, provisioning GPUs, configuring parallelism, and distributing workloads under tight latency, accuracy, and budget constraints. Exact optimization approaches (MILP) scale poorly for practical deployment scenarios.
AI.method: Proposes two constraint-aware heuristics: 1) Greedy Heuristic (GH) for single-pass allocation, and 2) Adaptive Greedy Heuristic (AGH) that enhances GH via multi-start construction, relocate-based local search, and GPU consolidation. Uses three constraint-aware mechanisms: TP-aware feasibility selection, cost-per-effective-coverage ranking, and TP upgrade.
AI.result: Both heuristics produce feasible solutions in under one second using Azure LLM Inference Trace workloads. AGH closely approaches optimal cost while achieving over 260x speedup on large-scale instances. Under stress tests with 1.5x parameter inflation, AGH maintains controlled SLO violations and stable cost, whereas exact solver placement degrades sharply.
AI.conclusion: Constraint-aware heuristic approaches provide practical, efficient solutions for LLM inference deployment at scale, outperforming exact optimization methods in terms of speed, scalability, and robustness to parameter changes while maintaining solution quality.

title: SEARL: Joint Optimization of Policy and Tool Graph Memory for Self-Evolving Agents
summary: Recent advances in Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) have demonstrated significant potential in single-turn reasoning tasks. With the paradigm shift toward self-evolving agentic learning, models are increasingly expected to learn from trajectories by synthesizing tools or accumulating explicit experiences. However, prevailing methods typically rely on large-scale LLMs or multi-agent frameworks, which hinder their deployment in resource-constrained environments. The inherent sparsity of outcome-based rewards also poses a substantial challenge, as agents typically receive feedback only upon completion of tasks. To address these limitations, we introduce a Tool-Memory based self-evolving agentic framework SEARL. Unlike approaches that directly utilize interaction experiences, our method constructs a structured experience memory that integrates planning with execution. This provides a novel state abstraction that facilitates generalization across analogous contexts, such as tool reuse. Consequently, agents extract explicit knowledge from historical data while leveraging inter-trajectory correlations to densify reward signals. We evaluate our framework on knowledge reasoning and mathematics tasks, demonstrating its effectiveness in achieving more practical and efficient learning.

AI.tldr: SEARL is a Tool-Memory based self-evolving agentic framework that addresses RLVR limitations by integrating planning with execution through structured experience memory for more practical and efficient learning in resource-constrained environments.
AI.motivation: Existing self-evolving agentic learning approaches rely on large LLMs or multi-agent frameworks that are unsuitable for resource-constrained environments. Additionally, outcome-based rewards are inherently sparse, providing feedback only upon task completion, which hinders efficient learning.
AI.method: SEARL introduces a Tool-Memory based framework that constructs structured experience memory integrating planning with execution. Unlike directly using interaction experiences, it creates a novel state abstraction enabling generalization across analogous contexts (like tool reuse). Agents extract explicit knowledge from historical data while leveraging inter-trajectory correlations to densify reward signals.
AI.result: The framework was evaluated on knowledge reasoning and mathematics tasks, demonstrating effectiveness in achieving more practical and efficient learning compared to prevailing methods.
AI.conclusion: SEARL addresses critical limitations in current RLVR approaches by providing a resource-efficient framework with densified reward signals through structured experience memory, enabling more practical deployment of self-evolving agentic learning in constrained environments.

title: Cluster Attention for Graph Machine Learning
summary: Message Passing Neural Networks have recently become the most popular approach to graph machine learning tasks; however, their receptive field is limited by the number of message passing layers. To increase the receptive field, Graph Transformers with global attention have been proposed; however, global attention does not take into account the graph topology and thus lacks graph-structure-based inductive biases, which are typically very important for graph machine learning tasks. In this work, we propose an alternative approach: cluster attention (CLATT). We divide graph nodes into clusters with off-the-shelf graph community detection algorithms and let each node attend to all other nodes in each cluster. CLATT provides large receptive fields while still having strong graph-structure-based inductive biases. We show that augmenting Message Passing Neural Networks or Graph Transformers with CLATT significantly improves their performance on a wide range of graph datasets including datasets from the recently introduced GraphLand benchmark representing real-world applications of graph machine learning.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes CLATT (cluster attention), a novel attention mechanism for graph machine learning that divides graph nodes into clusters using community detection algorithms, allowing nodes to attend to all other nodes within each cluster, providing large receptive fields while maintaining graph-structure inductive biases.
AI.motivation: Current approaches have limitations: Message Passing Neural Networks (MPNNs) have limited receptive fields constrained by the number of message passing layers, while Graph Transformers with global attention lack graph-structure-based inductive biases which are crucial for graph machine learning tasks.
AI.method: Proposes cluster attention (CLATT) which: 1) Uses off-the-shelf graph community detection algorithms to divide graph nodes into clusters, 2) Allows each node to attend to all other nodes within each cluster, 3) Can be integrated to augment both MPNNs and Graph Transformers.
AI.result: Augmenting both Message Passing Neural Networks and Graph Transformers with CLATT significantly improves their performance across a wide range of graph datasets, including datasets from the recently introduced GraphLand benchmark representing real-world applications.
AI.conclusion: CLATT provides an effective solution to balance receptive field size with graph-structure inductive biases, offering superior performance over existing approaches while being applicable to both MPNNs and Graph Transformers in various graph learning tasks.

title: Lightweight LLM Agent Memory with Small Language Models
summary: Although LLM agents can leverage tools for complex tasks, they still need memory to maintain cross-turn consistency and accumulate reusable information in long-horizon interactions. However, retrieval-based external memory systems incur low online overhead but suffer from unstable accuracy due to limited query construction and candidate filtering. In contrast, many systems use repeated large-model calls for online memory operations, improving accuracy but accumulating latency over long interactions. We propose LightMem, a lightweight memory system for better agent memory driven by Small Language Models (SLMs). LightMem modularizes memory retrieval, writing, and long-term consolidation, and separates online processing from offline consolidation to enable efficient memory invocation under bounded compute. We organize memory into short-term memory (STM) for immediate conversational context, mid-term memory (MTM) for reusable interaction summaries, and long-term memory (LTM) for consolidated knowledge, and uses user identifiers to support independent retrieval and incremental maintenance in multi-user settings. Online, LightMem operates under a fixed retrieval budget and selects memories via a two-stage procedure: vector-based coarse retrieval followed by semantic consistency re-ranking. Offline, it abstracts reusable interaction evidence and incrementally integrates it into LTM. Experiments show gains across model scales, with an average F1 improvement of about 2.5 on LoCoMo, more effective and low median latency (83 ms retrieval; 581 ms end-to-end).

AI.tldr: LightMem: A lightweight memory system for LLM agents using SLMs with three-tier memory organization and offline consolidation to improve efficiency and accuracy in long-horizon interactions.
AI.motivation: Existing memory systems for LLM agents face trade-offs: retrieval-based systems have low overhead but unstable accuracy due to limited query construction, while large-model approaches improve accuracy but accumulate latency over long interactions. There's a need for efficient, accurate memory systems that can maintain consistency and accumulate reusable information in long-term agent interactions.
AI.method: LightMem modularizes memory operations using Small Language Models (SLMs), separating online processing from offline consolidation. It organizes memory into three tiers: Short-Term Memory (STM) for immediate context, Mid-Term Memory (MTM) for reusable summaries, and Long-Term Memory (LTM) for consolidated knowledge. Online operations use vector-based coarse retrieval followed by semantic consistency re-ranking under fixed retrieval budget. Offline operations abstract reusable interaction evidence and incrementally integrate it into LTM.
AI.result: LightMem achieves average F1 improvement of about 2.5 on LoCoMo benchmark, with efficient performance: 83 ms retrieval latency and 581 ms end-to-end median latency. It shows gains across different model scales while maintaining low computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: LightMem demonstrates that lightweight memory systems using SLMs can effectively improve agent memory performance by balancing accuracy and efficiency through modular design, three-tier memory organization, and separation of online/offline operations, making it suitable for long-horizon interactions with bounded compute constraints.

title: SYN-DIGITS: A Synthetic Control Framework for Calibrated Digital Twin Simulation
summary: AI-based persona simulation -- often referred to as digital twin simulation -- is increasingly used for market research, recommender systems, and social sciences. Despite their flexibility, large language models (LLMs) often exhibit systematic bias and miscalibration relative to real human behavior, limiting their reliability. Inspired by synthetic control methods from causal inference, we propose SYN-DIGITS (SYNthetic Control Framework for Calibrated DIGItal Twin Simulation), a principled and lightweight calibration framework that learns latent structure from digital-twin responses and transfers it to align predictions with human ground truth. SYN-DIGITS operates as a post-processing layer on top of any LLM-based simulator and thus is model-agnostic. We develop a latent factor model that formalizes when and why calibration succeeds through latent space alignment conditions, and we systematically evaluate ten calibration methods across thirteen persona constructions, three LLMs, and two datasets. SYN-DIGITS supports both individual-level and distributional simulation for previously unseen questions and unobserved populations, with provable error guarantees. Experiments show that SYN-DIGITS achieves up to 50% relative improvements in individual-level correlation and 50--90% relative reductions in distributional discrepancy compared to uncalibrated baselines.

AI.tldr: SYN-DIGITS is a lightweight calibration framework that learns latent structure from AI persona simulations and transfers it to align predictions with real human behavior, reducing bias and improving accuracy for digital twin applications.
AI.motivation: While AI-based persona simulations (digital twins) are increasingly used in market research, recommender systems, and social sciences, large language models often exhibit systematic bias and miscalibration relative to real human behavior, limiting their reliability.
AI.method: SYN-DIGITS is a principled calibration framework inspired by synthetic control methods from causal inference. It operates as a post-processing layer on top of any LLM-based simulator, making it model-agnostic. The approach uses a latent factor model that formalizes when and why calibration succeeds through latent space alignment conditions.
AI.result: The framework was systematically evaluated across ten calibration methods, thirteen persona constructions, three LLMs, and two datasets. SYN-DIGITS supports both individual-level and distributional simulation for previously unseen questions and unobserved populations, with provable error guarantees. It achieves up to 50% relative improvements in individual-level correlation and 50-90% relative reductions in distributional discrepancy compared to uncalibrated baselines.
AI.conclusion: SYN-DIGITS provides an effective, lightweight calibration solution for AI-based persona simulations that significantly improves alignment with real human behavior while being model-agnostic and theoretically grounded with provable guarantees.

title: Agentivism: a learning theory for the age of artificial intelligence
summary: Learning theories have historically changed when the conditions of learning evolved. Generative and agentic AI create a new condition by allowing learners to delegate explanation, writing, problem solving, and other cognitive work to systems that can generate, recommend, and sometimes act on the learner's behalf. This creates a fundamental challenge for learning theory: successful performance can no longer be assumed to indicate learning. Learners may complete tasks effectively with AI support while developing less understanding, weaker judgment, and limited transferable capability. We argue that this problem is not fully captured by existing learning theories. Behaviourism, cognitivism, constructivism, and connectivism remain important, but they do not directly explain when AI-assisted performance becomes durable human capability. We propose Agentivism, a learning theory for human-AI interaction. Agentivism defines learning as durable growth in human capability through selective delegation to AI, epistemic monitoring and verification of AI contributions, reconstructive internalization of AI-assisted outputs, and transfer under reduced support. The importance of Agentivism lies in explaining how learning remains possible when intelligent delegation is easy and human-AI interaction is becoming a persistent and expanding part of human learning.

AI.tldr: A new learning theory called Agentivism is proposed to address how learning happens when learners can easily delegate cognitive work to AI systems, where successful task completion may not indicate actual learning.
AI.motivation: Generative and agentic AI create a new learning condition where learners can delegate cognitive work (explanation, writing, problem solving) to AI systems. This creates a fundamental challenge: successful performance with AI support may not indicate actual learning, as learners might develop less understanding, weaker judgment, and limited transferable capability.
AI.method: The paper argues that existing learning theories (behaviorism, cognitivism, constructivism, connectivism) don't fully explain when AI-assisted performance becomes durable human capability. The authors propose Agentivism as a new learning theory for human-AI interaction.
AI.result: Agentivism defines learning as durable growth in human capability through four key processes: 1) selective delegation to AI, 2) epistemic monitoring and verification of AI contributions, 3) reconstructive internalization of AI-assisted outputs, and 4) transfer under reduced support.
AI.conclusion: Agentivism explains how learning remains possible in an era where intelligent delegation to AI is easy and human-AI interaction is becoming a persistent and expanding part of human learning, addressing the gap left by traditional learning theories.

title: Learning Markov Processes as Sum-of-Square Forms for Analytical Belief Propagation
comment: Twenty-Ninth Annual Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Statistics (AISTATS 2026)
summary: Harnessing the predictive capability of Markov process models requires propagating probability density functions (beliefs) through the model. For many existing models however, belief propagation is analytically infeasible, requiring approximation or sampling to generate predictions. This paper proposes a functional modeling framework leveraging sparse Sum-of-Squares (SoS) forms for valid (conditional) density estimation. We study the theoretical restrictions of modeling conditional densities using the SoS form, and propose a novel functional form for addressing such limitations. The proposed architecture enables generalized simultaneous learning of basis functions and coefficients, while preserving analytical belief propagation. In addition, we propose a training method that allows for exact adherence to the normalization and non-negativity constraints. Our results show that the proposed method achieves accuracy comparable to state-of-the-art approaches while requiring significantly less memory in low-dimensional spaces, and it further scales to 12D systems when existing methods fail beyond 2D.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a functional modeling framework using sparse Sum-of-Squares forms for valid conditional density estimation, enabling analytical belief propagation while maintaining accuracy and scalability to higher dimensions.
AI.motivation: For many Markov process models, belief propagation is analytically infeasible, requiring approximation or sampling methods. There's a need for approaches that can perform analytical belief propagation while maintaining accuracy and scalability.
AI.method: The authors propose a functional modeling framework leveraging sparse Sum-of-Squares (SoS) forms for conditional density estimation. They address theoretical limitations of SoS forms, develop a novel functional architecture that enables simultaneous learning of basis functions and coefficients, and create a training method that enforces normalization and non-negativity constraints exactly.
AI.result: The method achieves accuracy comparable to state-of-the-art approaches while requiring significantly less memory in low-dimensional spaces. It scales to 12D systems, whereas existing methods fail beyond 2D dimensions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Sum-of-Squares framework provides an effective solution for analytical belief propagation in Markov process models, offering improved scalability and memory efficiency while maintaining competitive accuracy.

title: Automatic Generation of Executable BPMN Models from Medical Guidelines
summary: We present an end-to-end pipeline that converts healthcare policy documents into executable, data-aware Business Process Model and Notation (BPMN) models using large language models (LLMs) for simulation-based policy evaluation. We address the main challenges of automated policy digitization with four contributions: data-grounded BPMN generation with syntax auto-correction, executable augmentation, KPI instrumentation, and entropy-based uncertainty detection. We evaluate the pipeline on diabetic nephropathy prevention guidelines from three Japanese municipalities, generating 100 models per backend across three LLMs and executing each against 1,000 synthetic patients. On well-structured policies, the pipeline achieves a 100% ground-truth match with perfect per-patient decision agreement. Across all conditions, raw per-patient decision agreement exceeds 92%, and entropy scores increase monotonically with document complexity, confirming that the detector reliably separates unambiguous policies from those requiring targeted human clarification.

AI.tldr: End-to-end pipeline converts healthcare policy documents into executable BPMN models using LLMs, with automatic syntax correction, KPI instrumentation, and uncertainty detection for simulation-based policy evaluation.
AI.motivation: There's a need to automate the conversion of healthcare policy documents into executable process models for simulation-based policy evaluation, addressing challenges in policy digitization.
AI.method: Uses large language models (LLMs) to convert policy documents into Business Process Model and Notation (BPMN) models with four key components: data-grounded BPMN generation with syntax auto-correction, executable augmentation, KPI instrumentation, and entropy-based uncertainty detection.
AI.result: Evaluated on diabetic nephropathy prevention guidelines from three Japanese municipalities, generating 100 models per LLM backend across three LLMs. Achieved 100% ground-truth match with perfect per-patient decision agreement on well-structured policies, over 92% decision agreement across all conditions, and entropy scores that monotonically increase with document complexity.
AI.conclusion: The pipeline successfully automates healthcare policy digitization into executable models, with the uncertainty detector reliably identifying ambiguous policies requiring human clarification, enabling effective simulation-based policy evaluation.

title: Validated Synthetic Patient Generation for Small Longitudinal Cohorts: Coagulation Dynamics Across Pregnancy
summary: Small longitudinal clinical cohorts, common in maternal health, rare diseases, and early-phase trials, limit computational modeling: too few patients to train reliable models, yet too costly and slow to expand through additional enrollment. We present multiplicity-weighted Stochastic Attention (SA), a generative framework based on modern Hopfield network theory that addresses this gap. SA embeds real patient profiles as memory patterns in a continuous energy landscape and generates novel synthetic patients via Langevin dynamics that interpolate between stored patterns while preserving the geometry of the original cohort. Per-pattern multiplicity weights enable targeted amplification of rare clinical subgroups at inference time without retraining. We applied SA to a longitudinal coagulation dataset from 23 pregnant patients spanning 72 biochemical features across 3 visits (pre-pregnancy baseline, first trimester, and third trimester), including rare subgroups such as polycystic ovary syndrome and preeclampsia. Synthetic patients generated by SA were statistically, structurally, and mechanistically indistinguishable from their real counterparts across multiple independent validation tests, including an ordinary differential equation model of the coagulation cascade. A downstream utility test further showed that a mechanistic model calibrated entirely on synthetic patients predicted held-out real patient outcomes as well as one calibrated on real data. These results demonstrate that SA can produce clinically useful synthetic cohorts from very small longitudinal datasets, enabling data-augmented modeling in small-cohort settings.

AI.tldr: A generative framework called multiplicity-weighted Stochastic Attention (SA) generates clinically useful synthetic patients from small longitudinal cohorts by embedding real patient profiles in a continuous energy landscape and creating novel interpolations while preserving cohort geometry.
AI.motivation: Small longitudinal clinical cohorts (common in maternal health, rare diseases, and early-phase trials) have too few patients to train reliable models but are too costly and slow to expand through additional enrollment, limiting computational modeling.
AI.method: Multiplicity-weighted Stochastic Attention (SA), a generative framework based on modern Hopfield network theory that embeds real patient profiles as memory patterns in a continuous energy landscape and generates novel synthetic patients via Langevin dynamics that interpolate between stored patterns while preserving original cohort geometry, with per-pattern multiplicity weights enabling targeted amplification of rare clinical subgroups.
AI.result: Synthetic patients generated by SA were statistically, structurally, and mechanistically indistinguishable from real counterparts across multiple validation tests, including an ordinary differential equation model of the coagulation cascade. A mechanistic model calibrated entirely on synthetic patients predicted held-out real patient outcomes as well as one calibrated on real data.
AI.conclusion: SA can produce clinically useful synthetic cohorts from very small longitudinal datasets, enabling data-augmented modeling in small-cohort settings where traditional approaches are limited by insufficient data.

title: Silencing the Guardrails: Inference-Time Jailbreaking via Dynamic Contextual Representation Ablation
summary: While Large Language Models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable performance, they remain vulnerable to jailbreak attacks that circumvent safety constraints. Existing strategies, ranging from heuristic prompt engineering to computationally intensive optimization, often face significant trade-offs between effectiveness and efficiency. In this work, we propose Contextual Representation Ablation (CRA), a novel inference-time intervention framework designed to dynamically silence model guardrails. Predicated on the geometric insight that refusal behaviors are mediated by specific low-rank subspaces within the model's hidden states, CRA identifies and suppresses these refusal-inducing activation patterns during decoding without requiring expensive parameter updates or training. Empirical evaluation across multiple safety-aligned open-source LLMs demonstrates that CRA significantly outperforms baselines. These results expose the intrinsic fragility of current alignment mechanisms, revealing that safety constraints can be surgically ablated from internal representations, and underscore the urgent need for more robust defenses that secure the model's latent space.

AI.tldr: CRA (Contextual Representation Ablation) is an inference-time intervention framework that dynamically silences LLM guardrails by suppressing refusal-inducing activation patterns in low-rank subspaces, achieving state-of-the-art jailbreak effectiveness without training or parameter updates.
AI.motivation: LLMs remain vulnerable to jailbreak attacks despite their capabilities. Existing jailbreak methods face trade-offs between effectiveness and efficiency - some use heuristic prompt engineering that's not consistently effective, while others use computationally intensive optimization methods.
AI.method: CRA is based on the geometric insight that refusal behaviors are mediated by specific low-rank subspaces within LLM hidden states. The method identifies and suppresses these refusal-inducing activation patterns during decoding without requiring parameter updates or training. It's an inference-time intervention framework.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across multiple safety-aligned open-source LLMs shows CRA significantly outperforms baseline methods. The method exposes the intrinsic fragility of current alignment mechanisms by showing safety constraints can be surgically ablated from internal representations.
AI.conclusion: The results underscore the urgent need for more robust defenses that secure the model's latent space, revealing that current safety constraints can be effectively bypassed through targeted manipulation of internal representations without modifying model parameters.

title: Learning is Forgetting: LLM Training As Lossy Compression
comment: 12 page core paper, 16 page Appendix - A shorter version with fewer visuals appears at ICLR 2026
summary: Despite the increasing prevalence of large language models (LLMs), we still have a limited understanding of how their representational spaces are structured. This limits our ability to interpret how and what they learn or relate them to learning in humans. We argue LLMs are best seen as an instance of lossy compression, where over training they learn by retaining only information in their training data relevant to their objective(s). We show pre-training results in models that are optimally compressed for next-sequence prediction, approaching the Information Bottleneck bound on compression. Across an array of open weights models, each compresses differently, likely due to differences in the data and training recipes used. However even across different families of LLMs the optimality of a model's compression, and the information present in it, can predict downstream performance on across a wide array of benchmarks, letting us directly link representational structure to actionable insights about model performance. In the general case the work presented here offers a unified Information-Theoretic framing for how these models learn that is deployable at scale.

AI.tldr: LLMs function as lossy compressors - during pre-training, they approach Information Bottleneck optimal compression for sequence prediction, and the quality of this compression predicts downstream performance across various models.
AI.motivation: We lack understanding of how LLM representational spaces are structured, limiting our ability to interpret their learning processes or relate them to human learning. Current understanding is insufficient to explain what these models learn and how their internal representations develop.
AI.method: The paper frames LLMs as instances of lossy compression, analyzing how they retain only relevant information from training data for their objectives. It shows that pre-training approaches the Information Bottleneck bound on compression and examines compression patterns across various open-weights models with different data and training recipes.
AI.result: Models compress information differently due to variations in data and training, but across different LLM families, the optimality of compression and information content reliably predicts downstream performance on benchmarks. This establishes a direct link between representational structure and model performance.
AI.conclusion: LLMs learn through lossy compression that approaches Information Bottleneck optimality. This information-theoretic framing provides a unified understanding of how these models learn and allows for scalable analysis linking compression quality to practical performance metrics.

title: SPARD: Self-Paced Curriculum for RL Alignment via Integrating Reward Dynamics and Data Utility
summary: The evolution of Large Language Models (LLMs) is shifting the focus from single, verifiable tasks toward complex, open-ended real-world scenarios, imposing significant challenges on the post-training phase. In these settings, the scale and complexity of reward systems have grown significantly, transitioning toward multi-objective formulations that encompass a comprehensive spectrum of model capabilities and application contexts. However, traditional methods typically rely on fixed reward weights, ignoring non-stationary learning dynamics and struggling with data heterogeneity across dimensions. To address these issues, we propose SPARD, a framework that establishes an automated, self-paced curriculum by perceiving learning progress to dynamically adjust multi-objective reward weights and data importance, thereby synchronizing learning intent with data utility for optimal performance. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks demonstrate that SPARD significantly enhances model capabilities across all domains.

AI.tldr: SPARD is a framework that dynamically adjusts multi-objective reward weights and data importance in LLM post-training using self-paced curriculum learning based on learning progress.
AI.motivation: The evolution of LLMs toward complex, open-ended real-world scenarios challenges post-training methods. Traditional approaches use fixed reward weights, ignoring non-stationary learning dynamics and struggling with data heterogeneity across multiple objectives and dimensions.
AI.method: SPARD establishes an automated, self-paced curriculum by perceiving learning progress to dynamically adjust multi-objective reward weights and data importance, synchronizing learning intent with data utility.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks demonstrate that SPARD significantly enhances model capabilities across all domains.
AI.conclusion: The proposed SPARD framework effectively addresses limitations of traditional fixed-weight methods in complex LLM post-training scenarios by dynamically adapting to learning progress and data utility, leading to improved performance across multiple domains.

title: Implicit Regularization and Generalization in Overparameterized Neural Networks
summary:
