Code is available at: https://github.com/czg1225/DMax

AI.tldr: DMax is a new paradigm for diffusion language models that enables aggressive parallel decoding while preserving quality by reformulating decoding as progressive self-refinement from mask to token embeddings, using novel training and decoding techniques.
AI.motivation: Conventional masked diffusion language models suffer from error accumulation in parallel decoding, limiting their efficiency. The authors aim to enable more aggressive decoding parallelism while maintaining generation quality, which is crucial for practical deployment of efficient diffusion models.
AI.method: DMax introduces: 1) Progressive self-refinement from mask embeddings to token embeddings (instead of binary mask-to-token transition); 2) On-Policy Uniform Training that unifies masked and uniform diffusion language models training; 3) Soft Parallel Decoding that represents intermediate states as interpolations between predicted token and mask embeddings for iterative self-revision.
AI.result: DMax significantly improves throughput: TPF on GSM8K from 2.04 to 5.47 while preserving accuracy; on MBPP from 2.71 to 5.86 while maintaining comparable performance; achieves 1,338 TPS on two H200 GPUs at batch size 1. The method demonstrates effectiveness across various benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: DMax presents a successful paradigm for efficient diffusion language models that addresses error accumulation in parallel decoding, enabling substantial throughput improvements without sacrificing quality. The approach opens new possibilities for practical deployment of diffusion-based language models through better training and decoding strategies.

title: Dead Weights, Live Signals: Feedforward Graphs of Frozen Language Models
summary: We present a feedforward graph architecture in which heterogeneous frozen large language models serve as computational nodes, communicating through a shared continuous latent space via learned linear projections. Building on recent work demonstrating geometric compatibility between independently trained LLM latent spaces~\cite{armstrong2026thinking}, we extend this finding from static two-model steering to end-to-end trainable multi-node graphs, where projection matrices are optimized jointly via backpropagation through residual stream injection hooks. Three small frozen models (Llama-3.2-1B, Qwen2.5-1.5B, Gemma-2-2B) encode the input into a shared latent space whose aggregate signal is injected into two larger frozen models (Phi-3-mini, Mistral-7B), whose representations feed a lightweight cross-attention output node. With only 17.6M trainable parameters against approximately 12B frozen, the architecture achieves 87.3\% on ARC-Challenge, 82.8\% on OpenBookQA, and 67.2\% on MMLU, outperforming the best single constituent model by 11.4, 6.2, and 1.2 percentage points respectively, and outperforming parameter-matched learned classifiers on frozen single models by 9.1, 5.2, and 6.7 points. Gradient flow through multiple frozen model boundaries is empirically verified to be tractable, and the output node develops selective routing behavior across layer-2 nodes without explicit supervision.

AI.tldr: A graph architecture connects frozen LLMs via learnable projections in shared latent space, achieving strong few-shot reasoning with minimal trainable parameters.
AI.motivation: To create computationally efficient systems that leverage multiple frozen LLMs by connecting them through geometric compatibility in their latent spaces, enabling effective few-shot reasoning with minimal training.
AI.method: Feedforward graph architecture with heterogeneous frozen LLMs as nodes, communicating through shared continuous latent space via learned linear projections. Small models encode input into shared latent space, whose aggregate signal is injected into larger models, feeding a lightweight cross-attention output node. Only 17.6M parameters trained while ~12B remain frozen.
AI.result: Achieves 87.3% on ARC-Challenge (11.4 pts better than best single model), 82.8% on OpenBookQA (6.2 pts better), and 67.2% on MMLU (1.2 pts better). Also outperforms parameter-matched learned classifiers by 9.1, 5.2, and 6.7 points respectively.
AI.conclusion: Frozen LLMs can be effectively combined via trainable projections in shared latent space, enabling strong few-shot reasoning with minimal training. Gradient flow through multiple frozen models is tractable, and the system develops selective routing behavior without explicit supervision.

title: Leveraging Complementary Embeddings for Replay Selection in Continual Learning with Small Buffers
summary: Catastrophic forgetting remains a key challenge in Continual Learning (CL). In replay-based CL with severe memory constraints, performance critically depends on the sample selection strategy for the replay buffer. Most existing approaches construct memory buffers using embeddings learned under supervised objectives. However, class-agnostic, self-supervised representations often encode rich, class-relevant semantics that are overlooked. We propose a new method, Multiple Embedding Replay Selection, MERS, which replaces the buffer selection module with a graph-based approach that integrates both supervised and self-supervised embeddings. Empirical results show consistent improvements over SOTA selection strategies across a range of continual learning algorithms, with particularly strong gains in low-memory regimes. On CIFAR-100 and TinyImageNet, MERS outperforms single-embedding baselines without adding model parameters or increasing replay volume, making it a practical, drop-in enhancement for replay-based continual learning.

AI.tldr: MERS: A graph-based replay buffer selection method that integrates both supervised and self-supervised embeddings to improve continual learning performance, especially in low-m8emory settings.
AI.motivation: Current replay-based continual learning methods rely on supervised embeddings for buffer selection, overlooking the rich class-relevant semantics encoded in class-agnostic self-supervised representations. This leads to suboptimal sample selection, especially under severe memory constraints.
AI.method: MERS (Multiple Embedding Replay Selection) replaces traditional buffer selection with a graph-based approach that integrates both supervised and self-supervised embeddings. It uses these multiple embeddings to construct a more informative selection strategy without adding model parameters or increasing replay volume.
AI.result: MERS shows consistent improvements over state-of-the-art selection strategies across various continual learning algorithms, with particularly strong gains in low-memory regimes. On CIFAR-100 and TinyImageNet benchmarks, it outperforms single-embedding baselines.
AI.conclusion: MERS provides a practical, drop-in enhancement for replay-based continual learning by effectively leveraging both supervised and self-supervised representations for improved sample selection, addressing catastrophic forgetting more effectively than existing methods.

title: EgoEverything: A Benchmark for Human Behavior Inspired Long Context Egocentric Video Understanding in AR Environment
summary: Long context egocentric video understanding has recently attracted significant research attention, with augmented reality (AR) highlighted as one of its most important application domains. Nevertheless, the task remains highly challenging due to the need for reasoning over extended temporal contexts and diverse, unstructured activities. Although several benchmarks exist, most egocentric datasets rely on human worn cameras and focus mainly on visual content, with limited consideration of underlying user behavior when forming video-related queries. EgoEverything is a benchmark that explicitly considers human behavior by leveraging human attention signals, abstracted from gaze data, when generating questions. It comprises over 5,000 multiple choice question answer pairs, spanning more than 100 hours of video. By integrating human attention signals during question generation, it more faithfully captures natural human behavior and offers a realistic evaluation setting for long-context egocentric video understanding in AR.

AI.tldr: EgoEverything is a new benchmark for long-context egocentric video understanding that incorporates human attention signals from gaze data when generating questions, creating more realistic evaluation for AR applications.
AI.motivation: Current egocentric video understanding benchmarks are limited because they mainly focus on visual content from human-worn cameras without considering underlying user behavior when forming video-related queries. Existing datasets lack integration of natural human attention patterns, which is crucial for realistic evaluation in AR applications.
AI.method: The authors create the EgoEverything benchmark by leveraging human attention signals abstracted from gaze data during question generation. This approach explicitly considers human behavior patterns, resulting in over 5,000 multiple choice question-answer pairs spanning more than 100 hours of egocentric video.
AI.result: The benchmark successfully captures natural human behavior more faithfully by integrating attention signals, providing a more realistic evaluation setting for long-context egocentric video understanding, particularly for AR applications.
AI.conclusion: EgoEverything addresses key limitations in existing benchmarks by incorporating human attention data, offering a more realistic and behaviorally-grounded evaluation framework for long-context egocentric video understanding in augmented reality domains.

title: Bias-Constrained Diffusion Schedules for PDE Emulations: Reconstruction Error Minimization and Efficient Unrolled Training
summary: Conditional Diffusion Models are powerful surrogates for emulating complex spatiotemporal dynamics, yet they often fail to match the accuracy of deterministic neural emulators for high-precision tasks. In this work, we address two critical limitations of autoregressive PDE diffusion models: their sub-optimal single-step accuracy and the prohibitive computational cost of unrolled training. First, we characterize the relationship between the noise schedule, the reconstruction error reduction rate and the diffusion exposure bias, demonstrating that standard schedules lead to suboptimal reconstruction error. Leveraging this insight, we propose an \textit{Adaptive Noise Schedule} framework that minimizes inference reconstruction error by dynamically constraining the model's exposure bias. We further show that this optimized schedule enables a fast \textit{Proxy Unrolled Training} method to stabilize long-term rollouts without the cost of full Markov Chain sampling. Both proposed methods enable significant improvements in short-term accuracy and long-term stability over diffusion and deterministic baselines on diverse benchmarks, including forced Navier-Stokes, Kuramoto-Sivashinsky and Transonic Flow.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes two improvements for conditional diffusion models in PDE emulation: an Adaptive Noise Schedule to reduce reconstruction error and a Proxy Unrolled Training method to stabilize long-term predictions without expensive sampling.
AI.motivation: Conditional diffusion models struggle to match deterministic neural emulators for high-precision spatiotemporal tasks due to sub-optimal single-step accuracy and prohibitive computational costs of unrolled training.
AI.method: 1) Characterizes relationship between noise schedule, reconstruction error, and diffusion exposure bias; 2) Proposes Adaptive Noise Schedule framework to minimize inference reconstruction error by constraining exposure bias; 3) Introduces Proxy Unrolled Training method that leverages the optimized schedule for stable long-term rollouts without full Markov Chain sampling.
AI.result: Significant improvements in short-term accuracy and long-term stability over both diffusion and deterministic baselines on diverse benchmarks including forced Navier-Stokes, Kuramoto-Sivashinsky, and Transonic Flow.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Adaptive Noise Schedule and Proxy Unrolled Training address critical limitations of autoregressive PDE diffusion models, enabling them to achieve competitive accuracy with deterministic emulators while maintaining computational efficiency for long-term predictions.

title: Scaling-Aware Data Selection for End-to-End Autonomous Driving Systems
comment: Accepted to CVPR 2026, 8 pages of main body and 10 pages of appendix
summary: Large-scale deep learning models for physical AI applications depend on diverse training data collection efforts. These models and correspondingly, the training data, must address different evaluation criteria necessary for the models to be deployable in real-world environments. Data selection policies can guide the development of the training set, but current frameworks do not account for the ambiguity in how data points affect different metrics. In this work, we propose Mixture Optimization via Scaling-Aware Iterative Collection (MOSAIC), a general data selection framework that operates by: (i) partitioning the dataset into domains; (ii) fitting neural scaling laws from each data domain to the evaluation metrics; and (iii) optimizing a data mixture by iteratively adding data from domains that maximize the change in metrics. We apply MOSAIC to autonomous driving (AD), where an End-to-End (E2E) planner model is evaluated on the Extended Predictive Driver Model Score (EPDMS), an aggregate of driving rule compliance metrics. Here, MOSAIC outperforms a diverse set of baselines on EPDMS with up to 80\% less data.

AI.tldr: MOSAIC is a data selection framework that uses scaling laws and iterative domain selection to optimize training data mixtures, achieving 80% less data usage for autonomous driving applications while improving performance metrics.
AI.motivation: Current data selection policies for training large-scale AI models don't account for ambiguity in how data points affect different evaluation metrics, especially in physical AI applications like autonomous driving where models must meet multiple deployment criteria.
AI.method: MOSAIC operates by: 1) partitioning dataset into domains, 2) fitting neural scaling laws from each domain to evaluation metrics, and 3) iteratively adding data from domains that maximize metric improvements. Applied to autonomous driving with an E2E planner evaluated on EPDMS driving compliance metrics.
AI.result: MOSAIC outperforms diverse baselines on the Extended Predictive Driver Model Score (EPDMS) with up to 80% less training data than conventional approaches.
AI.conclusion: MOSAIC provides an effective framework for data selection that accounts for metric ambiguity through domain partitioning and scaling laws, enabling more efficient training data collection for complex physical AI systems like autonomous driving.

title: SOLAR: Communication-Efficient Model Adaptation via Subspace-Oriented Latent Adapter Reparametrization
summary: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods, such as LoRA, enable scalable adaptation of foundation models by injecting low-rank adapters. However, their communication and storage costs remain a major bottleneck in resource-constrained settings. We propose SOLAR (Subspace-Oriented Latent Adapter Reparameterization), a post-training compression framework that substantially reduces the communication cost (i.e., the number of parameters to transmit or store) of PEFT adapters. SOLAR expresses each PEFT update as a linear combination of basis vectors formed from the foundation model's singular vectors with controlled random perturbations. By exploiting the subspace similarity (the alignment of principal directions) between the foundation model and task-specific fine-tuned updates, SOLAR decouples the adapter size from PEFT structure and ensures compact yet expressive representations. It is model-agnostic and compatible with existing PEFT methods, including LoRA, AdaLoRA, and other adapter modules. We theoretically establish a bound on the reconstruction error. Experiments on language and vision tasks using LLaMA, GPT, and ViT models demonstrate that SOLAR preserves task performance while significantly reducing model representation sizes, offering an effective and communication-efficient solution for deployment in distributed systems and edge devices.

AI.tldr: SOLAR compresses PEFT adapters by representing them as linear combinations of perturbed singular vectors from the foundation model, reducing communication/storage costs while preserving performance.
AI.motivation: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods like LoRA have high communication and storage costs that create bottlenecks in resource-constrained settings like distributed systems and edge devices.
AI.method: SOLAR expresses each PEFT update as a linear combination of basis vectors formed from the foundation model's singular vectors with controlled random perturbations, exploiting subspace similarity between foundation models and fine-tuned updates.
AI.result: SOLAR significantly reduces model representation sizes while preserving task performance on language and vision tasks using LLaMA, GPT, and ViT models, with theoretical bounds on reconstruction error.
AI.conclusion: SOLAR provides an effective, communication-efficient solution for deploying fine-tuned models in distributed systems and edge devices, with model-agnostic compatibility across existing PEFT methods.

title: ADAPTive Input Training for Many-to-One Pre-Training on Time-Series Classification
summary: Recent work on time-series models has leveraged self-supervised training to learn meaningful features and patterns in order to improve performance on downstream tasks and generalize to unseen modalities. While these pretraining methods have shown great promise in one-to-many scenarios, where a model is pre-trained on one dataset and fine-tuned on a downstream dataset, they have struggled to generalize to new datasets when more datasets are added during pre-training. This is a fundamental challenge in building foundation models for time-series data, as it limits the ability to develop models that can learn from a large variety of diverse datasets available. To address this challenge, we present a new pre-training paradigm for time-series data called ADAPT, which can efficiently align the physical properties of data in the time-series domain, enabling mixed-batch pre-training despite the extreme discrepancies in the input sizes and channel dimensions of pre-training data. We trained on 162 time-series classification datasets and set new state-of-the-art performance for classification benchmarks. We successfully train a model within the time-series domain on a wide range of datasets simultaneously, which is a major building block for building generalist foundation models in time-series domains.

AI.tldr: ADAPT is a new pre-training paradigm for time-series that enables mixed-batch training across diverse datasets by aligning physical properties, achieving SOTA on classification benchmarks with 162 datasets.
AI.motivation: Current time-series pre-training methods struggle to generalize when multiple diverse datasets are used during pre-training, limiting the development of foundation models that can learn from varied time-series data sources.
AI.method: ADAPT efficiently aligns the physical properties of time-series data in the time-series domain, enabling mixed-batch pre-training despite extreme discrepancies in input sizes and channel dimensions across different datasets.
AI.result: Trained on 162 time-series classification datasets, ADAPT sets new state-of-the-art performance for classification benchmarks and successfully trains a model on a wide range of datasets simultaneously.
AI.conclusion: ADAPT represents a major building block for building generalist foundation models in time-series domains by enabling simultaneous training across diverse datasets, overcoming previous limitations in multi-dataset pre-training.

title: Zero-shot Multivariate Time Series Forecasting Using Tabular Prior Fitted Networks
summary: Tabular foundation models, particularly Prior-data Fitted Networks like TabPFN have emerged as the leading contender in a myriad of tasks ranging from data imputation to label prediction on the tabular data format surpassing the historical successes of tree-based models. This has led to investigations on their applicability to forecasting time series data which can be formulated as a tabular problem. While recent work to this end has displayed positive results, most works have limited their treatment of multivariate time series problems to several independent univariate time series forecasting subproblems, thus ignoring any inter-channel interactions. Overcoming this limitation, we introduce a generally applicable framework for multivariate time series forecasting using tabular foundation models. We achieve this by recasting the multivariate time series forecasting problem as a series of scalar regression problems which can then be solved zero-shot by any tabular foundation model with regression capabilities. We present results of our method using the TabPFN-TS backbone and compare performance with the current state of the art tabular methods.

AI.tldr: Tabular foundation models like TabPFN can now handle multivariate time series forecasting by treating it as a series of scalar regression problems, enabling zero-shot forecasting while preserving inter-channel interactions.
AI.motivation: Tabular foundation models have shown superior performance on tabular data tasks compared to traditional tree-based models. While they've been applied to time series forecasting (which can be formulated as tabular problems), existing approaches typically treat multivariate time series as independent univariate problems, ignoring important inter-channel interactions between different time series variables.
AI.method: The authors introduce a framework that recasts multivariate time series forecasting as a series of scalar regression problems. This formulation allows any tabular foundation model with regression capabilities (specifically TabPFN-TS) to solve these problems zero-shot, capturing the interactions between different channels/variables in the multivariate time series.
AI.result: The paper presents results using the TabPFN-TS backbone and compares performance with current state-of-the-art tabular methods for time series forecasting, though specific metrics or comparisons are not detailed in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully extends tabular foundation models to multivariate time series forecasting while preserving inter-channel interactions, overcoming limitations of previous approaches that treated multivariate problems as independent univariate tasks.

title: Adversarial Label Invariant Graph Data Augmentations for Out-of-Distribution Generalization
comment: 21 pages, 3 figures, accepted at ICML SCIS 2023
summary: Out-of-distribution (OoD) generalization occurs when representation learning encounters a distribution shift. This occurs frequently in practice when training and testing data come from different environments. Covariate shift is a type of distribution shift that occurs only in the input data, while the concept distribution stays invariant. We propose RIA - Regularization for Invariance with Adversarial training, a new method for OoD generalization under convariate shift. Motivated by an analogy to $Q$-learning, it performs an adversarial exploration for training data environments. These new environments are induced by adversarial label invariant data augmentations that prevent a collapse to an in-distribution trained learner. It works with many existing OoD generalization methods for covariate shift that can be formulated as constrained optimization problems. We develop an alternating gradient descent-ascent algorithm to solve the problem, and perform extensive experiments on OoD graph classification for various kinds of synthetic and natural distribution shifts. We demonstrate that our method can achieve high accuracy compared with OoD baselines.

AI.tldr: RIA (Regularization for Invariance with Adversarial training) is a new method for out-of-distribution generalization under covariate shift that uses adversarial exploration of training data environments via label-invariant augmentations to prevent collapse to in-distribution learning.
AI.motivation: Out-of-distribution generalization is important when training and testing data come from different environments. The paper focuses on covariate shift (input distribution shifts while concept distribution stays invariant), which frequently occurs in practice and poses challenges for representation learning.
AI.method: RIA performs adversarial exploration of training data environments using adversarial label-invariant data augmentations. The method works with existing OoD generalization methods for covariate shift formulated as constrained optimization problems, using an alternating gradient descent-ascent algorithm. It's motivated by an analogy to Q-learning to explore training data environments.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on OoD graph classification for various synthetic and natural distribution shifts demonstrate that RIA achieves high accuracy compared with OoD baselines. The method effectively prevents collapse to in-distribution trained learners.
AI.conclusion: RIA provides an effective approach for out-of-distribution generalization under covariate shift by using adversarial label-invariant data augmentations to explore training environments, preventing overfitting to in-distribution patterns and improving generalization to unseen distributions.

title: KV Cache Offloading for Context-Intensive Tasks
summary: With the growing demand for long-context LLMs across a wide range of applications, the key-value (KV) cache has become a critical bottleneck for both latency and memory usage. Recently, KV-cache offloading has emerged as a promising approach to reduce memory footprint and inference latency while preserving accuracy. Prior evaluations have largely focused on tasks that do not require extracting large amounts of information from the context. In this work, we study KV-cache offloading on context-intensive tasks: problems where the solution requires looking up a lot of information from the input prompt. We create and release the Text2JSON benchmark, a highly context-intensive task that requires extracting structured knowledge from raw text. We evaluate modern KV offloading on Text2JSON and other context-intensive tasks and find significant performance degradation on both Llama 3 and Qwen 3 models. Our analysis identifies two key reasons for poor accuracy: low-rank projection of keys and unreliable landmarks, and proposes a simpler alternative strategy that significantly improves accuracy across multiple LLM families and benchmarks. These findings highlight the need for a comprehensive and rigorous evaluation of long-context compression techniques.

AI.tldr: KV-cache offloading techniques suffer significant accuracy degradation on context-intensive tasks requiring large information extraction, leading to a new benchmark and improved alternative strategy.
AI.motivation: Current KV-cache offloading evaluations focus on tasks that don't require extracting large amounts of information from context, but with growing demand for long-context LLMs, context-intensive tasks present new challenges where existing offloading techniques fail.
AI.method: Created the Text2JSON benchmark for context-intensive tasks, evaluated modern KV offloading on it and other context-intensive tasks using Llama 3 and Qwen 3 models, identified failure reasons, and proposed an improved alternative strategy.
AI.result: Found significant performance degradation in KV-cache offloading on context-intensive tasks, identified two key failure reasons (low-rank key projection and unreliable landmarks), and developed a simpler alternative strategy that significantly improves accuracy across multiple LLM families and benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Existing KV-cache offloading techniques are inadequate for context-intensive tasks, highlighting the need for more comprehensive evaluation of long-context compression techniques and providing a new benchmark and improved approach for better performance.

title: Provably Adaptive Linear Approximation for the Shapley Value and Beyond
summary: The Shapley value, and its broader family of semi-values, has received much attention in various attribution problems. A fundamental and long-standing challenge is their efficient approximation, since exact computation generally requires an exponential number of utility queries in the number of players $n$. To meet the challenges of large-scale applications, we explore the limits of efficiently approximating semi-values under a $Θ(n)$ space constraint. Building upon a vector concentration inequality, we establish a theoretical framework that enables sharper query complexities for existing unbiased randomized algorithms. Within this framework, we systematically develop a linear-space algorithm that requires $O(\frac{n}{ε^{2}}\log\frac{1}δ)$ utility queries to ensure $P(\|\hat{\boldsymbolφ}-\boldsymbolφ\|_{2}\geqε)\leq δ$ for all commonly used semi-values. In particular, our framework naturally bridges OFA, unbiased kernelSHAP, SHAP-IQ and the regression-adjusted approach, and definitively characterizes when paired sampling is beneficial. Moreover, our algorithm allows explicit minimization of the mean square error for each specific utility function. Accordingly, we introduce the first adaptive, linear-time, linear-space randomized algorithm, Adalina, that theoretically achieves improved mean square error. All of our theoretical findings are experimentally validated.

AI.tldr: Efficient linear-space approximation of semi-values (including Shapley value) with improved query complexity and adaptive algorithms.
AI.motivation: Semi-values like Shapley value are important for attribution problems but face exponential computation challenges in large-scale applications. There's a need for efficient approximation under space constraints.
AI.method: Developed theoretical framework using vector concentration inequality to analyze unbiased randomized algorithms. Created linear-space algorithm with O(n/ε² log(1/δ)) utility queries. Introduced adaptive algorithm Adalina that minimizes mean square error adaptively.
AI.result: Achieved sharper query complexity bounds, unified existing approaches (OFA, kernelSHAP, SHAP-IQ, regression-adjusted), characterized when paired sampling helps. Experimental validation of theoretical findings.
AI.conclusion: Provides efficient linear-space approximation for semi-values with theoretical guarantees and practical adaptive algorithm, addressing fundamental scalability challenges in attribution problems.

title: Less Approximates More: Harmonizing Performance and Confidence Faithfulness via Hybrid Post-Training for High-Stakes Tasks
summary: Large language models are increasingly deployed in high-stakes tasks, where confident yet incorrect inferences may cause severe real-world harm, bringing the previously overlooked issue of confidence faithfulness back to the forefront. A promising solution is to jointly optimize unsupervised Reinforcement Learning from Internal Feedback (RLIF) with reasoning-trace-guided Reasoning Distillation (RD), which may face three persistent challenges: scarcity of high-quality training corpora, factually unwarranted overconfidence and indiscriminate fusion that amplifies erroneous updates. Inspired by the human confidence accumulation from uncertainty to certainty, we propose Progressive Reasoning Gain (PRG) to measure whether reasoning steps progressively strengthen support for the final answer. Furthermore, we introduce HyTuning, a hybrid post-training framework that adaptively reweights RD and RLIF via a PRG-style metric, using scarce supervised reasoning traces as a stable anchor while exploiting abundant unlabeled queries for scalability. Experiments on several domain-specific and general benchmarks demonstrate that HyTuning improves accuracy while achieving confidence faithfulness under limited supervision, supporting a practical "Less Approximates More" effect.

AI.tldr: HyTuning is a hybrid post-training framework that combines reasoning distillation and reinforcement learning with internal feedback to improve confidence faithfulness in LLMs by using progressive reasoning gain metrics to adaptively weight training components.
AI.motivation: LLMs deployed in high-stakes tasks can cause severe harm with confident but incorrect inferences, highlighting the need for confidence faithfulness. Existing approaches face challenges: scarce high-quality training data, factually unwarranted overconfidence, and indiscriminate fusion that amplifies errors.
AI.method: Introduces Progressive Reasoning Gain (PRG) to measure if reasoning steps progressively strengthen support for final answers. Proposes HyTuning, a hybrid framework that adaptively reweights Reasoning Distillation (RD) and Reinforcement Learning from Internal Feedback (RLIF) using PRG-style metrics, leveraging scarce supervised reasoning traces as anchors while exploiting abundant unlabeled queries for scalability.
AI.result: Experiments on domain-specific and general benchmarks show HyTuning improves accuracy while achieving confidence faithfulness under limited supervision, demonstrating a "Less Approximates More" effect where limited supervision approximates fully-supervised performance.
AI.conclusion: HyTuning effectively addresses confidence faithfulness in LLMs by measuring progressive reasoning gains and adaptively combining reasoning distillation with reinforcement learning, achieving practical performance with limited supervision through a hybrid training approach.

title: A Machine Learning Framework for Turbofan Health Estimation via Inverse Problem Formulation
summary: Estimating the health state of turbofan engines is a challenging ill-posed inverse problem, hindered by sparse sensing and complex nonlinear thermodynamics. Research in this area remains fragmented, with comparisons limited by the use of unrealistic datasets and insufficient exploration of the exploitation of temporal information. This work investigates how to recover component-level health indicators from operational sensor data under realistic degradation and maintenance patterns. To support this study, we introduce a new dataset that incorporates industry-oriented complexities such as maintenance events and usage changes. Using this dataset, we establish an initial benchmark that compares steady-state and nonstationary data-driven models, and Bayesian filters, classic families of methods used to solve this problem. In addition to this benchmark, we introduce self-supervised learning (SSL) approaches that learn latent representations without access to true health labels, a scenario reflective of real-world operational constraints. By comparing the downstream estimation performance of these unsupervised representations against the direct prediction baselines, we establish a practical lower bound on the difficulty of solving this inverse problem. Our results reveal that traditional filters remain strong baselines, while SSL methods reveal the intrinsic complexity of health estimation and highlight the need for more advanced and interpretable inference strategies. For reproducibility, both the generated dataset and the implementation used in this work are made accessible.

AI.tldr: A study on turbofan engine health estimation using a new industry-realistic dataset compares steady-state/nonstationary models, Bayesian filters, and self-supervised learning approaches, revealing traditional filters as strong baselines and highlighting the complexity of unsupervised health indicator recovery.
AI.motivation: Turbofan engine health estimation is a challenging ill-posed inverse problem due to sparse sensing and complex thermodynamics. Current research is fragmented with unrealistic datasets and insufficient exploration of temporal information exploitation.
AI.method: 1) Introduces new industry-oriented dataset with maintenance events and usage changes; 2) Establishes benchmark comparing steady-state vs nonstationary data-driven models and Bayesian filters; 3) Introduces self-supervised learning approaches that learn latent representations without true health labels; 4) Compares downstream estimation performance against direct prediction baselines.
AI.result: Traditional filters remain strong baselines for health estimation. SSL methods reveal intrinsic complexity of the problem and highlight need for more advanced, interpretable inference strategies. Provides practical lower bound on difficulty of solving this inverse problem.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates the challenge of recovering component-level health indicators from operational sensor data under realistic conditions. While traditional filters perform well, the complexity revealed by SSL methods suggests need for more sophisticated approaches, with both dataset and implementation made available for reproducibility.

title: TTVS: Boosting Self-Exploring Reinforcement Learning via Test-time Variational Synthesis
summary: Despite significant advances in Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) driven by reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), this paradigm is fundamentally limited in specialized or novel domains where such supervision is prohibitively expensive or unavailable, posing a key challenge for test-time adaptation. While existing test-time methods offer a potential solution, they are constrained by learning from static query sets, risking overfitting to textual patterns. To address this gap, we introduce Test-Time Variational Synthesis (TTVS), a novel framework that enables LRMs to self-evolve by dynamically augmenting the training stream from unlabeled test queries. TTVS comprises two synergistic modules: (1) Online Variational Synthesis, which transforms static test queries into a dynamic stream of diverse, semantically-equivalent variations, enforcing the model to learn underlying problem logic rather than superficial patterns; (2) Test-time Hybrid Exploration, which balances accuracy-driven exploitation with consistency-driven exploration across synthetic variants. Extensive experiments show TTVS yields superior performance across eight model architectures. Notably, using only unlabeled test-time data, TTVS not only surpasses other test-time adaptation methods but also outperforms state-of-the-art supervised RL-based techniques trained on vast, high-quality labeled data.

AI.tldr: TTVS enables Large Reasoning Models to self-evolve at test time by dynamically synthesizing diverse variations from unlabeled test queries, achieving superior performance without expensive supervision.
AI.motivation: Current Large Reasoning Models rely on reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards, which is prohibitively expensive in specialized or novel domains where such supervision is unavailable. Existing test-time methods risk overfitting to textual patterns because they learn from static query sets.
AI.method: Test-Time Variational Synthesis (TTVS) with two modules: (1) Online Variational Synthesis that transforms static test queries into dynamic streams of semantically-equivalent variations, and (2) Test-time Hybrid Exploration that balances accuracy-driven exploitation with consistency-driven exploration across synthetic variants.
AI.result: TTVS yields superior performance across eight model architectures. Using only unlabeled test-time data, it surpasses other test-time adaptation methods and outperforms state-of-the-art supervised RL-based techniques trained on vast labeled data.
AI.conclusion: TTVS effectively addresses the limitations of expensive supervision in specialized domains by enabling LRMs to self-evolve through dynamic query augmentation, learning underlying problem logic rather than superficial patterns, and achieving better performance than supervised approaches.

title: Persistence-Augmented Neural Networks
summary: Topological Data Analysis (TDA) provides tools to describe the shape of data, but integrating topological features into deep learning pipelines remains challenging, especially when preserving local geometric structure rather than summarizing it globally. We propose a persistence-based data augmentation framework that encodes local gradient flow regions and their hierarchical evolution using the Morse-Smale complex. This representation, compatible with both convolutional and graph neural networks, retains spatially localized topological information across multiple scales. Importantly, the augmentation procedure itself is efficient, with computational complexity $O(n \log n)$, making it practical for large datasets. We evaluate our method on histopathology image classification and 3D porous material regression, where it consistently outperforms baselines and global TDA descriptors such as persistence images and landscapes. We also show that pruning the base level of the hierarchy reduces memory usage while maintaining competitive performance. These results highlight the potential of local, structured topological augmentation for scalable and interpretable learning across data modalities.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an efficient persistence-based data augmentation framework using Morse-Smale complex to encode local gradient flow regions, enabling scalable integration of topological features into deep learning pipelines while preserving local geometric structure.
AI.motivation: Topological Data Analysis (TDA) provides tools to describe data shape, but integrating topological features into deep learning remains challenging, particularly when trying to preserve local geometric structure rather than just summarizing it globally.
AI.method: The authors propose a persistence-based data augmentation framework that encodes local gradient flow regions and their hierarchical evolution using the Morse-Smale complex. This representation is compatible with both convolutional and graph neural networks and retains spatially localized topological information across multiple scales. The method has O(n log n) computational complexity.
AI.result: The method outperforms baselines and global TDA descriptors (persistence images and landscapes) on histopathology image classification and 3D porous material regression tasks. Pruning the base level of the hierarchy reduces memory usage while maintaining competitive performance.
AI.conclusion: The results demonstrate the potential of local, structured topological augmentation for scalable and interpretable learning across different data modalities, addressing the challenge of integrating topological features while preserving local geometric structure in deep learning pipelines.

title: Quantization Impact on the Accuracy and Communication Efficiency Trade-off in Federated Learning for Aerospace Predictive Maintenance
summary: Federated learning (FL) enables privacy-preserving predictive maintenance across distributed aerospace fleets, but gradient communication overhead constrains deployment on bandwidth-limited IoT nodes. This paper investigates the impact of symmetric uniform quantization ($b \in \{32,8,4,2\}$ bits) on the accuracy--efficiency trade-off of a custom-designed lightweight 1-D convolutional model (AeroConv1D, 9\,697 parameters) trained via FL on the NASA C-MAPSS benchmark under a realistic Non-IID client partition. Using a rigorous multi-seed evaluation ($N=10$ seeds), we show that INT4 achieves accuracy \emph{statistically indistinguishable} from FP32 on both FD001 ($p=0.341$) and FD002 ($p=0.264$ MAE, $p=0.534$ NASA score) while delivering an $8\times$ reduction in gradient communication cost (37.88~KiB $\to$ 4.73~KiB per round). A key methodological finding is that naïve IID client partitioning artificially suppresses variance; correct Non-IID evaluation reveals the true operational instability of extreme quantization, demonstrated via a direct empirical IID vs.\ Non-IID comparison. INT2 is empirically characterized as unsuitable: while it achieves lower MAE on FD002 through extreme quantization-induced over-regularization, this apparent gain is accompanied by catastrophic NASA score instability (CV\,=\,45.8\% vs.\ 22.3\% for FP32), confirming non-reproducibility under heterogeneous operating conditions. Analytical FPGA resource projections on the Xilinx ZCU102 confirm that INT4 fits within hardware constraints (85.5\% DSP utilization), potentially enabling a complete FL pipeline on a single SoC. The full simulation codebase and FPGA estimation scripts are publicly available at https://github.com/therealdeadbeef/aerospace-fl-quantization.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that 4-bit quantization (INT4) enables efficient federated learning for aerospace predictive maintenance without accuracy loss, while revealing the importance of realistic Non-IID evaluation to properly assess quantization stability.
AI.motivation: Federated learning enables privacy-preserving predictive maintenance for distributed aerospace fleets, but gradient communication overhead limits deployment on bandwidth-constrained IoT nodes. There's a need to balance model accuracy with communication efficiency.
AI.method: The study investigates symmetric uniform quantization (32, 8, 4, 2 bits) on a lightweight 1-D convolutional model (AeroConv1D, 9,697 parameters) using FL on the NASA C-MAPSS benchmark. Key methodological innovation: rigorous Non-IID client partitioning (vs. naïve IID) with multi-seed evaluation (N=10 seeds). Also includes FPGA resource analysis on Xilinx ZCU102.
AI.result: INT4 achieves accuracy statistically indistinguishable from FP32 on FD001 (p=0.341) and FD002 (p=0.264 MAE, p=0.534 NASA score) while reducing gradient communication by 8× (37.88 KiB → 4.73 KiB per round). INT2 shows unstable performance with catastrophic NASA score instability (CV=45.8% vs 22.3% for FP32). Non-IID evaluation revealed true operational instability masked by IID partitioning.
AI.conclusion: INT4 quantization enables efficient federated learning for aerospace predictive maintenance without accuracy loss, fitting within FPGA hardware constraints. Realistic Non-IID evaluation is crucial for proper assessment, and INT2 is unsuitable due to irreproducible performance. The approach enables complete FL pipelines on single SoCs.

title: The Impact of Dimensionality on the Stability of Node Embeddings
summary: Previous work has established that neural network-based node embeddings return different outcomes when trained with identical parameters on the same dataset, just from using different training seeds. Yet, it has not been thoroughly analyzed how key hyperparameters such as embedding dimension could impact this instability. In this work, we investigate how varying the dimensionality of node embeddings influences both their stability and downstream performance. We systematically evaluate five widely used methods -- ASNE, DGI, GraphSAGE, node2vec, and VERSE -- across multiple datasets and embedding dimensions. We assess stability from both a representational perspective and a functional perspective, alongside performance evaluation. Our results show that embedding stability varies significantly with dimensionality, but we observe different patterns across the methods we consider: while some approaches, such as node2vec and ASNE, tend to become more stable with higher dimensionality, other methods do not exhibit the same trend. Moreover, we find that maximum stability does not necessarily align with optimal task performance. These findings highlight the importance of carefully selecting embedding dimension, and provide new insights into the trade-offs between stability, performance, and computational effectiveness in graph representation learning.

AI.tldr: Neural network node embedding stability varies significantly with dimensionality, but trends differ across methods - some become more stable with higher dimensions while others don't, and maximum stability doesn't align with optimal task performance.
AI.motivation: Previous work shows neural network-based node embeddings return different outcomes with identical parameters due to different training seeds, but there's been no thorough analysis of how key hyperparameters like embedding dimension impact this instability.
AI.method: Systematically evaluate five node embedding methods (ASNE, DGI, GraphSAGE, node2vec, VERSE) across multiple datasets and embedding dimensions, assessing stability from both representational and functional perspectives alongside performance evaluation.
AI.result: Embedding stability varies significantly with dimensionality with different patterns across methods: node2vec and ASNE tend to become more stable with higher dimensionality, while other methods don't show this trend. Maximum stability doesn't necessarily align with optimal task performance.
AI.conclusion: The findings highlight the importance of carefully selecting embedding dimension and provide new insights into trade-offs between stability, performance, and computational effectiveness in graph representation learning.

title: What Drives Representation Steering? A Mechanistic Case Study on Steering Refusal
summary: Applying steering vectors to large language models (LLMs) is an efficient and effective model alignment technique, but we lack an interpretable explanation for how it works-- specifically, what internal mechanisms steering vectors affect and how this results in different model outputs. To investigate the causal mechanisms underlying the effectiveness of steering vectors, we conduct a comprehensive case study on refusal. We propose a multi-token activation patching framework and discover that different steering methodologies leverage functionally interchangeable circuits when applied at the same layer. These circuits reveal that steering vectors primarily interact with the attention mechanism through the OV circuit while largely ignoring the QK circuit-- freezing all attention scores during steering drops performance by only 8.75% across two model families. A mathematical decomposition of the steered OV circuit further reveals semantically interpretable concepts, even in cases where the steering vector itself does not. Leveraging the activation patching results, we show that steering vectors can be sparsified by up to 90-99% while retaining most performance, and that different steering methodologies agree on a subset of important dimensions.

AI.tldr: Steering vectors in LLMs primarily work through the OV circuit of attention mechanisms, not the QK circuit, and can be sparsely applied (90-99% reduction) while maintaining performance.
AI.motivation: There's a lack of interpretable explanation for how steering vectors work in large language models - specifically, what internal mechanisms they affect and how this leads to different model outputs. The authors want to understand the causal mechanisms behind steering vector effectiveness.
AI.method: The authors conduct a comprehensive case study on refusal behavior and propose a multi-token activation patching framework. They analyze how different steering methodologies affect model circuits, particularly examining the OV (output-value) and QK (query-key) circuits in attention mechanisms. They also perform mathematical decomposition of the steered OV circuit.
AI.result: Different steering methodologies leverage functionally interchangeable circuits when applied at the same layer. Steering vectors primarily interact with the attention mechanism through the OV circuit while largely ignoring the QK circuit (freezing attention scores only drops performance by 8.75%). The OV circuit decomposition reveals semantically interpretable concepts, and steering vectors can be sparsified by 90-99% while retaining most performance, with different methodologies agreeing on important dimensions.
AI.conclusion: Steering vectors in LLMs work primarily through sparse interventions in the OV circuit of attention mechanisms, revealing interpretable semantic concepts and enabling significant sparsification while maintaining effectiveness, suggesting a common underlying mechanism across different steering approaches.

title: Meta-learning In-Context Enables Training-Free Cross Subject Brain Decoding
summary: Visual decoding from brain signals is a key challenge at the intersection of computer vision and neuroscience, requiring methods that bridge neural representations and computational models of vision. A field-wide goal is to achieve generalizable, cross-subject models. A major obstacle towards this goal is the substantial variability in neural representations across individuals, which has so far required training bespoke models or fine-tuning separately for each subject. To address this challenge, we introduce a meta-optimized approach for semantic visual decoding from fMRI that generalizes to novel subjects without any fine-tuning. By simply conditioning on a small set of image-brain activation examples from the new individual, our model rapidly infers their unique neural encoding patterns to facilitate robust and efficient visual decoding. Our approach is explicitly optimized for in-context learning of the new subject's encoding model and performs decoding by hierarchical inference, inverting the encoder. First, for multiple brain regions, we estimate the per-voxel visual response encoder parameters by constructing a context over multiple stimuli and responses. Second, we construct a context consisting of encoder parameters and response values over multiple voxels to perform aggregated functional inversion. We demonstrate strong cross-subject and cross-scanner generalization across diverse visual backbones without retraining or fine-tuning. Moreover, our approach requires neither anatomical alignment nor stimulus overlap. This work is a critical step towards a generalizable foundation model for non-invasive brain decoding.

AI.tldr: Meta-optimized fMRI decoding that generalizes to new subjects without fine-tuning by using in-context learning of individual neural encoding patterns.
AI.motivation: Addressing the challenge of substantial variability in neural representations across individuals, which currently requires training bespoke models for each subject, to achieve generalizable cross-subject visual decoding from brain signals.
AI.method: Meta-optimized approach that conditions on a small set of image-brain activation examples from new individuals, using hierarchical inference: 1) estimating per-voxel visual response encoder parameters across brain regions, 2) aggregated functional inversion using encoder parameters and response values across multiple voxels.
AI.result: Demonstrates strong cross-subject and cross-scanner generalization across diverse visual backbones without retraining or fine-tuning, requiring neither anatomical alignment nor stimulus overlap.
AI.conclusion: This represents a critical step toward a generalizable foundation model for non-invasive brain decoding that can rapidly adapt to new individuals through in-context learning.
